function.arguments: {"question": "In RCTs of workplace/work-related stress, what were the effects of CBT on sickness absence/return-to-work and symptom outcomes (stress, burnout/exhaustion, depression/anxiety)? Extract any quantitative results if available.", "text_name_focus_list": ["asplund2018internetbasedstressmanagement pages 1-2", "asplund2018internetbasedstressmanagement pages 6-7", "ebert2016selfguidedinternetbasedand pages 1-2", "ebert2016selfguidedinternetbasedand pages 7-8", "lindsater2018internetbasedcognitivebehavioral pages 1-2", "asplund2023workfocusedversusgeneric pages 1-2", "salomonsson2020effectsofcognitive pages 1-2", "ruwaard2007e\u2010mailedstandardizedcognitive pages 1-4"]}
function.name: gather_evidence

messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000024

From text 'salomonsson2020effectsofcognitive pages 1-2'.

Sigrid Salomonsson, Fredrik Santoft, Elin Lindsäter, Kersti Ejeby, Martin Ingvar, Brjánn Ljótsson, Lars‐Göran Öst, Mats Lekander, and Erik Hedman‐Lagerlöf. Effects of cognitive behavioural therapy and return-to-work intervention for patients on sick leave due to stress-related disorders: results from a randomized trial. Scandinavian journal of psychology, 61:281-289, Nov 2020. URL: https://doi.org/10.1111/sjop.12590, doi:10.1111/sjop.12590. This article has 50 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

This randomized trial (N = 211; CBT n=64, RTW-I n=67, COMBO n=80) analyzed a Stress subgroup (adjustment or exhaustion disorder; n = 152) with primary outcomes of Clinician Severity Rating (CSR) and registry sick-leave data. Subgroup analyses found that, for stress-related disorders, CBT produced greater symptom reduction than the return-to-work intervention (RTW-I) at posttreatment, while the combined intervention (COMBO) did not differ from either CBT or RTW-I. Despite symptom benefits, there were no differences between treatments on sick-leave outcomes within the Stress subgroup. An exploratory subgroup (patients with depression, anxiety or insomnia) suggested RTW-I reduced sick leave faster than CBT. The authors conclude CBT may be promising for stress and exhaustion disorder, but note prior meta-analyses have generally found no clear treatment effects for clinical stress/exhaustion and only small effects of psychological interventions on sick leave for common mental disorders. The excerpt does not provide numerical effect sizes or statistical estimates for symptom or sick-leave outcomes.


---

## pqac-00000025

From text 'xu2024theeffectivenessof pages 21-22'.

Huaying Xu, Jinxuan Cai, Rakshat Sawhney, Stephen Jiang, Nicholas Buys, and Jing Sun. The effectiveness of cognitive-behavioral therapy in helping people on sick leave to return to work: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Journal of Occupational Rehabilitation, pages 1-33, Apr 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/s10926-023-10116-4, doi:10.1007/s10926-023-10116-4. This article has 37 citations and is from a domain leading peer-reviewed journal.

In the included RCTs of CBT for work-related/sick-leave populations, CBT reduced sickness absence days and increased return-to-work. Across 15 studies (n=1727) CBT reduced sick-leave days by a mean of −3.649 (95% CI −5.253 to −2.046; p<0.001) with a moderate effect size (−0.395). Across 16 studies (n=2298) CBT increased number returning to work (mean difference 1.5, 95% CI 1.019–1.722; p<0.05). Heterogeneity was high for sick-leave days (I2=92.991) but low/non-significant for RTW (I2=32.998). Symptom outcomes categorized as “mental illness” (5 studies, n=1301) showed a small improvement (MD −0.810) and a small effect size (−0.196) per Table 2. Subgroup analyses found larger sick-leave reductions when interventions were face-to-face (MD −8.673), included stress-management components (MD −3.498), used homework (MD −2.615), mood-management (effect size −0.926), longer courses (≥16 weeks) or longer sessions (≥90 min), combined CBT (MD −14.785), or group delivery. The excerpt does not separately report burnout/exhaustion or detailed depression/anxiety-specific estimates beyond the “mental illness” category.


---

## pqac-00000026

From text 'asplund2023workfocusedversusgeneric pages 1-2'.

Robert Persson Asplund, Sofia Asplund, Helene von Buxhoeveden, Hanna Delby, Karin Eriksson, Maurits Svenning Gerhardsson, Joachim Palm, Thea Skyttberg, Julia Torstensson, Brjánn Ljótsson, Per Carlbring, and Gerhard Andersson. Work-focused versus generic internet-based interventions for employees with stress-related disorders: randomized controlled trial. Journal of Medical Internet Research, 25:e34446, Apr 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.2196/34446, doi:10.2196/34446. This article has 25 citations and is from a domain leading peer-reviewed journal.

This randomized trial compared a work-focused internet CBT (W-iCBT), a generic iCBT, and a waitlist control in employees with stress-related disorders. Both W-iCBT and generic iCBT produced large reductions in burnout (primary outcome, measured by SMBQ): posttreatment Cohen's d = 1.00 (W-iCBT) and 0.83 (iCBT), and at 6 months both d = 0.74. Secondary health- and work-related outcomes showed moderate-to-large effects overall. Importantly, only the work-focused intervention (W-iCBT) produced significant improvements in work ability and reduced short-term sickness absence: 445 fewer short-term sickness absence days versus waitlist and 324 fewer days versus generic iCBT. There were no significant differences in work experience or long-term sick leave. The excerpt does not provide separate quantitative results for depression or anxiety outcomes beyond the general statement about secondary health outcomes, so symptom-specific effect sizes for depression/anxiety are not available here.


---

## pqac-00000027

From text 'xu2024theeffectivenessof pages 27-28'.

Huaying Xu, Jinxuan Cai, Rakshat Sawhney, Stephen Jiang, Nicholas Buys, and Jing Sun. The effectiveness of cognitive-behavioral therapy in helping people on sick leave to return to work: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Journal of Occupational Rehabilitation, pages 1-33, Apr 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/s10926-023-10116-4, doi:10.1007/s10926-023-10116-4. This article has 37 citations and is from a domain leading peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt reports results from a meta-analysis of CBT-based interventions for employees on sick leave. Overall the authors state CBT "significantly increased the number of people who RTW and improved physical function," though no RCT-only RTW/sick-leave numeric estimate is provided in the excerpt. Depression outcomes (15 studies, n=2870) showed a mean reduction of −0.965 (95% CI −1.745 to −0.186; p<0.05) and a small standardized effect size of −0.176 (95% CI −0.322 to −0.031; p<0.05). Another reported mean reduction (unspecified outcome in excerpt) was −0.810 (95% CI −1.561 to −0.060; p<0.05) with SMD −0.196 (95% CI −0.308 to −0.083; p<0.01). Subgroup data for RCTs (13 RCTs, n≈2676) show small but significant post–pre depression effects (reported in table as MD ≈ −0.175 and SMD ≈ −0.154). Publication-bias testing (Egger) was non-significant for sick-leave reduced days (p=0.098) and overall minimal (p>0.05). The excerpt provides clear quantitative evidence of small improvements in depression/anxiety symptoms and a qualitative statement of increased RTW, but it lacks RCT-specific numeric estimates for RTW/sick-leave and for burnout/exhaustion or stress outcomes.


---

## pqac-00000028

From text 'xu2024theeffectivenessof pages 1-2'.

Huaying Xu, Jinxuan Cai, Rakshat Sawhney, Stephen Jiang, Nicholas Buys, and Jing Sun. The effectiveness of cognitive-behavioral therapy in helping people on sick leave to return to work: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Journal of Occupational Rehabilitation, pages 1-33, Apr 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/s10926-023-10116-4, doi:10.1007/s10926-023-10116-4. This article has 37 citations and is from a domain leading peer-reviewed journal.

This systematic review/meta-analysis pooled 34 RCTs of CBT-based interventions for employees on sick leave. Fifteen RCTs (n=1727) reported sickness absence and found a mean reduction in sick-leave days of −3.654 (95% CI −5.253 to −2.046; p<0.001). Sixteen papers (n=2298) reported return-to-work timing, with the intervention group returning about 1.5 days earlier (95% CI 1.019 to 1.722; p<0.05). Symptom outcomes: the authors report CBT-based interventions were effective for fatigue, mental illness, and depression and improved physical function, but showed no effects for stress, anxiety, or working ability. The review targeted studies of employees on sick leave for causes including depression, stress-related mental disorders, and chronic pain; the excerpt does not isolate results exclusively from RCTs of workplace/work-related stress, so direct applicability to workplace-specific stress trials is limited in this passage.


---

## pqac-00000029

From text 'joyce2016workplaceinterventionsfor pages 8-9'.

Sadhbh Joyce, Matthew Modini, H. Christensen, A. Mykletun, R. Bryant, P. Mitchell, and S. Harvey. Workplace interventions for common mental disorders: a systematic meta-review. Psychological Medicine, 46:683-697, Dec 2016. URL: https://doi.org/10.1017/s0033291715002408, doi:10.1017/s0033291715002408. This article has 712 citations and is from a highest quality peer-reviewed journal.

Across included reviews of workplace interventions, CBT shows consistent symptom reduction for common mental disorders (rated highly: "Symptom reduction: ⋆⋆⋆"). Reviews report significant improvements in anxiety and depression symptomology and promising effects as an early secondary intervention for reducing work-related stress. However, improvements in clinical symptoms did not reliably translate into work-related outcomes in general workplace stress trials (e.g. "this did not translate to notable improvements in work-related factors such as absenteeism and productivity"). Where occupational outcomes improve, it is typically when CBT is delivered as part of a return-to-work (RTW) programme or when therapy is explicitly work-focused (e.g. problem-focused CBT). One review reported a quantitative RTW benefit: CBT may facilitate an earlier RTW by an average of 2 weeks among employees with adjustment disorder. Overall, the evidence base is mixed in quality; some reviews concluded that study quality was low and prevented firm recommendations about organizational outcomes.


---

## pqac-00000030

From text 'ebert2016selfguidedinternetbasedand pages 1-2'.

David Daniel Ebert, Elena Heber, Matthias Berking, Heleen Riper, Pim Cuijpers, Burkhardt Funk, and Dirk Lehr. Self-guided internet-based and mobile-based stress management for employees: results of a randomised controlled trial. Occupational and Environmental Medicine, 73:315-323, Feb 2016. URL: https://doi.org/10.1136/oemed-2015-103269, doi:10.1136/oemed-2015-103269. This article has 232 citations and is from a highest quality peer-reviewed journal.

This randomized trial (N=264; PSS-10 ≥22) evaluated a self-guided internet/mobile stress-management intervention (iSMI: seven sessions + booster, including problem-solving and emotion-regulation techniques) versus a 6-month wait-list control. The primary outcome was perceived stress (PSS-10). The iSMI produced a large reduction in perceived stress at post-treatment (7 weeks) with d=0.96 (95% CI 0.70–1.21) and a moderate effect at 6 months, d=0.65 (95% CI 0.40–0.89). Secondary outcomes showed significant small-to-moderate improvements in depression, anxiety, emotional exhaustion (burnout-related), sleep problems, worrying, mental health–related quality of life, psychological detachment, emotion-regulation skills, and presenteeism in favor of the iSMI. However, at 6 months, absenteeism (sickness absence) did not differ significantly between groups, nor did work engagement or physical health–related quality of life. In sum, this trial shows meaningful symptom improvements (stress, burnout-related emotional exhaustion, depression/anxiety) but no detected effect on absenteeism at 6 months.


---

## pqac-00000031

From text 'xu2024theeffectivenessof pages 2-3'.

Huaying Xu, Jinxuan Cai, Rakshat Sawhney, Stephen Jiang, Nicholas Buys, and Jing Sun. The effectiveness of cognitive-behavioral therapy in helping people on sick leave to return to work: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Journal of Occupational Rehabilitation, pages 1-33, Apr 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/s10926-023-10116-4, doi:10.1007/s10926-023-10116-4. This article has 37 citations and is from a domain leading peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt summarizes a systematic review/meta-analysis protocol assessing RCTs of CBT for employees on sick leave, focusing on return-to-work (RTW) and days of sick leave as primary outcomes and symptoms (stress, anxiety, depression, fatigue, working ability, physical function) as secondary outcomes. Inclusion required RCTs of CBT with both cognitive and behavioral components in adult employees on workplace-related sick leave. The text reports mixed trial findings: some RCTs showed CBT significantly reduced duration of musculoskeletal (MSK)-related sick leaves and improved physical and mental health, and related interventions also showed benefits for RTW after mental-condition leaves (stress, burnout). However, other RCTs found no significant difference between CBT and care-as-usual despite improvements versus no-treatment controls. The excerpt does not provide quantitative effect sizes, confidence intervals, or pooled estimates — only qualitative statements about significance and inconsistency across studies. Searches covered multiple databases for studies from 1990 to mid-2022.

(2) Linds\u00e4ter et al. 2018 therapist-guided iCBT for adjustment/exhaustion disorder: large perceived-stress reduction vs waitlist (d=1.09) maintained at 6 months; no work ability/functional impairment effect. (3) Ruwaard et al. 2007 e-mail CBT for work-related stress: significant improvements vs waitlist, with within-group effects ~d 0.5\u20130.9 and between-group effects ~d 0.1\u20130.6; no work outcomes reported. messages.content:
Updated plan:
{
  "objectives": [
    {
      "id": 1,
      "objective": "Define scope and key outcomes for CBT targeting workplace stress (e.g., perceived stress, burnout, anxiety/depression, absenteeism/work functioning) and inclusion criteria (peer-reviewed, working populations, CBT or CBT-based interventions).",
      "rationale": "Clarifies what counts as CBT for workplace stress and what ‘effectiveness’ means so evidence extraction is aligned with the user’s question.",
      "status": "completed",
      "result": "Scope defined as CBT or CBT-based stress management interventions delivered to working adults with work-related stress complaints or stress-related disorders (including internet-delivered CBT), with effectiveness judged on validated symptom outcomes (perceived stress, burnout/exhaustion, depression/anxiety) and occupational outcomes (sick leave/return-to-work, work ability, productivity/presenteeism).",
      "evaluation": "Establishes operational definitions and outcome domains used in subsequent evidence gathering."
    },
    {
      "id": 2,
      "objective": "Identify high-quality peer-reviewed syntheses (systematic reviews/meta-analyses) on CBT for work-related stress and related occupational outcomes.",
      "rationale": "Syntheses provide the most reliable estimate of effectiveness and typical effect sizes across studies.",
      "status": "completed",
      "result": "Key syntheses identified and extracted: (1) Richardson & Rothstein 2008 meta-analysis of workplace stress-management interventions: overall moderate effect (d≈0.53) with high heterogeneity; few studies measured absenteeism/productivity. (2) Klink et al. 2001 meta-analysis: CBT and multimodal interventions show moderate effects; organizational interventions largely non-significant. (3) Bhui et al. 2012 review of reviews: CBT programs tend to show larger individual-level mental-health effects than other approaches; evidence gaps for absenteeism. (4) Joyce et al. 2016 meta-review: consistent symptom reduction for CBT-based workplace interventions; occupational outcomes mixed and better when CBT is work-focused/RTW-oriented. (5) Ryan et al. 2017 systematic review of web-based workplace stress interventions: CBT/iCBT shows moderate-to-large effects on stress but inconsistent/limited effects on absenteeism. (6) Xu et al. 2024 meta-analysis of CBT for employees on sick leave: pooled reductions in sick-leave days (~3.7 days) and ~1.5 days earlier RTW; no pooled effect on stress/anxiety or working ability; small improvement in depression.",
      "evaluation": "Multiple peer-reviewed reviews/meta-analyses relevant to CBT for workplace stress and occupational outcomes were obtained, with quantitative estimates extracted where available."
    },
    {
      "id": 3,
      "objective": "Identify and extract evidence from key randomized controlled trials of CBT delivered in workplace/occupational contexts (face-to-face, group, online/iCBT), including outcomes and follow-up durability.",
      "rationale": "RCTs provide direct causal evidence and allow characterization of intervention formats and magnitude/duration of benefit.",
      "status": "completed",
      "result": "Extracted RCT evidence emphasizing occupational stress samples and stress-related disorders: (1) Ebert et al. 2016 self-guided iSMI (CBT-based) in employees with elevated stress: large stress reduction post (d=0.96) and moderate at 6 months (d=0.65), plus improvements in emotional exhaustion and depression/anxiety; no absenteeism effect at 6 months. (2) Lindsäter et al. 2018 therapist-guided iCBT for adjustment/exhaustion disorder: large perceived-stress reduction vs waitlist (d=1.09) maintained at 6 months; no work ability/functional impairment effect. (3) Ruwaard et al. 2007 e-mail CBT for work-related stress: significant improvements vs waitlist, with within-group effects ~d 0.5–0.9 and between-group effects ~d 0.1–0.6; no work outcomes reported. (4) Asplund et al. 2023 employees with stress-related disorders: work-focused iCBT and generic iCBT both reduced burnout strongly (post d=1.00 and 0.83; 6-month d=0.74), and only work-focused iCBT improved work ability and reduced short-term sickness absence (hundreds of days fewer). (5) Salomonsson et al. 2020 sick-listed stress subgroup: CBT reduced symptoms more than RTW intervention posttreatment but did not reduce sick leave relative to RTW intervention.",
      "evaluation": "Includes multiple peer-reviewed RCTs in working populations and stress-related disorders with quantitative effect sizes and follow-up where available, covering both symptom and work outcomes."

function.arguments: {"artifact_type": "table", "description": "Create a compact markdown table summarizing key peer-reviewed evidence on CBT for workplace/work-related stress. Include rows for: Richardson & Rothstein 2008 (meta-analysis), van der Klink/Klink et al. 2001 (meta-analysis), Bhui et al. 2012 (review of reviews), Joyce et al. 2016 (meta-review), Ryan et al. 2017 (systematic review web-based), Xu et al. 2024 (RTW meta-analysis), and key RCTs: Ebert 2016, Linds\u00e4ter 2018, Ruwaard 2007, Asplund 2023, Salomonsson 2020. Columns: Study type/population, CBT format, Main outcomes, Quantitative effect (effect sizes/MD), Durability/follow-up, Notes on work outcomes."}
function.name: create_artifact

messages.content:
### Artifact (ID artifact-00)

| Study | Study type / population | CBT format | Main outcomes | Quantitative effect | Durability / follow-up | Notes on work outcomes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Richardson & Rothstein 2008 | Meta-analysis of occupational stress-management interventions in workers | CBT, multimodal, relaxation, organizational programs | Overall stress/mental-health outcomes | Overall pooled effect d≈0.53; high heterogeneity (I²≈73%); few absenteeism/productivity studies (pqac-00000015) | Not summarized as a single follow-up estimate in excerpt | Supports moderate overall benefit of stress-management interventions, but evidence on absenteeism/productivity is sparse (pqac-00000015) |
| van der Klink / Klink et al. 2001 | Meta-analysis of 48 experimental studies of work-related stress interventions | Cognitive-behavioural, multimodal, relaxation, organizational | Complaints, psychological resources/responses, quality of work life | CBT and multimodal interventions showed moderate effects; organizational interventions non-significant; excerpt does not provide exact pooled d by CBT subgroup (pqac-00000013) | Not specified in excerpt | Stronger evidence for individual than organizational outcomes; work-outcome estimates not detailed (pqac-00000013) |
| Bhui et al. 2012 | Review of 23 systematic reviews / 499 primary studies | Individual interventions including CBT | Anxiety, depression, absenteeism | CBT programs repeatedly showed larger individual-level mental-health effects than other approaches; no numeric pooled CBT estimate in excerpt (pqac-00000014) | Review-level synthesis; no single durability estimate | Evidence for absenteeism is limited; organizational interventions mixed (pqac-00000014) |
| Joyce et al. 2016 | Systematic meta-review of workplace interventions for common mental disorders | CBT-based workplace and RTW-focused interventions | Symptom reduction, absenteeism, productivity, RTW | Consistent symptom reduction; one cited Cochrane review suggested earlier RTW by ~2 weeks; occupational effects otherwise mixed (pqac-00000009, pqac-00000029) | Depends on underlying reviews | Work-focused / RTW-oriented CBT appears more likely to improve occupational outcomes than generic CBT (pqac-00000009, pqac-00000029) |
| Ryan et al. 2017 | Systematic review of web-based workplace stress interventions | Web-based CBT / iCBT / blended CBT | Stress, mental health, insomnia, work functioning | Across trials, effects ranged from about d=0.3 to >1.0; examples include perceived stress d=0.96 post and d=0.65 at 6 months in GET.ON Stress-related trials (pqac-00000011, pqac-00000016) | Some studies reported 6-month maintenance; uncontrolled 3-year follow-up in one trial (pqac-00000011, pqac-00000022) | Work effects inconsistent; many studies found no absenteeism/presenteeism benefit, though some showed faster RTW or small work-performance gains (pqac-00000011, pqac-00000016, pqac-00000021) |
| Xu et al. 2024 | Systematic review and meta-analysis of 34 RCTs in employees on sick leave | CBT-based interventions, including stress-management and RTW-oriented variants | Sick leave, RTW, depression, stress/anxiety, work ability | Sick leave reduced by MD −3.654 days (95% CI −5.253 to −2.046); RTW ~1.5 days earlier; depression small benefit (effect sizes ~−0.176 to −0.196); no pooled effect for stress, anxiety, or working ability (pqac-00000008, pqac-00000010, pqac-00000025, pqac-00000028, pqac-00000032) | Pooled across varied follow-up intervals; figure/table extracted from review (pqac-00000032, pqac-00000033) | Best quantitative evidence for occupational outcomes, but heterogeneity is high for sick-leave reduction (pqac-00000025, pqac-00000032) |
| Ebert 2016 | RCT in employees with elevated perceived stress (N=264) | Self-guided internet/mobile CBT-based stress management | Perceived stress, depression/anxiety, emotional exhaustion, presenteeism, absenteeism | Perceived stress d=0.96 post, d=0.65 at 6 months; secondary outcomes improved with small-to-moderate effects (pqac-00000018, pqac-00000030) | 6-month follow-up with maintained moderate stress benefit (pqac-00000018, pqac-00000030) | Improved presenteeism, but no significant absenteeism effect at 6 months (pqac-00000018, pqac-00000030) |
| Lindsäter 2018 | RCT in clinical stress-related disorders / adjustment or exhaustion disorder (N=100) | Therapist-guided internet CBT | Perceived stress, secondary stress-related symptoms, work ability | Large stress reduction vs waitlist, d=1.09; moderate-to-large secondary symptom improvements (pqac-00000005, pqac-00000020) | Effects maintained at 6 months (pqac-00000005, pqac-00000020) | No significant effect on work ability or functional impairment (pqac-00000005, pqac-00000020) |
| Ruwaard 2007 | RCT in adults with work-related stress (n=239 randomized) | 7-week e-mailed standardized CBT | Stress, depression, anxiety, emotional exhaustion | Within-group effects ~d=0.5–0.9; between-group effects ~d=0.1–0.6, largest for stress (pqac-00000004, pqac-00000023) | Follow-up suggested more pronounced effects, but attrition limited interpretation (pqac-00000004, pqac-00000023) | No work-specific outcomes reported in extracted sections (pqac-00000004, pqac-00000023) |
| Asplund 2023 | RCT in employees with stress-related disorders (N=182) | Work-focused iCBT vs generic iCBT vs waitlist | Burnout, work ability, sickness absence | Burnout post: d=1.00 (work-focused) and 0.83 (generic); both d=0.74 at 6 months; only work-focused iCBT improved work ability and reduced short-term sickness absence (445 fewer days vs waitlist; 324 fewer vs generic iCBT) (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000017, pqac-00000026) | 6-month benefits maintained; 12-month assessment mentioned in trial (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000017) | Strong evidence that adding work-focused components may improve short-term occupational outcomes beyond symptom relief (pqac-00000017, pqac-00000026) |
| Salomonsson 2020 | Randomized trial in primary-care patients on sick leave; stress subgroup with adjustment/exhaustion disorder (n=152 within larger N=211 trial) | Face-to-face CBT vs RTW intervention vs combined | Symptom severity, registry sick leave | CBT reduced symptoms more than RTW intervention posttreatment; no numeric effect size in excerpt; no sick-leave difference between groups (pqac-00000019, pqac-00000024) | Posttreatment and registry follow-up reported in trial, but no compact numeric durability estimate in excerpt | Illustrates common pattern: symptom benefit without clear added sick-leave benefit for CBT alone (pqac-00000019, pqac-00000024) |


*Table: This table summarizes major peer-reviewed reviews and randomized trials on CBT for workplace or work-related stress. It highlights that CBT generally improves stress and related symptoms, while work outcomes such as absenteeism and return-to-work improve less consistently unless interventions are explicitly work-focused.*

Artifact IDs that may be injected into the answer: [artifact-00]

Context IDs that may be cited: [pqac-00000000, pqac-00000001, pqac-00000002, pqac-00000003, pqac-00000004, pqac-00000005, pqac-00000006, pqac-00000007, pqac-00000008, pqac-00000009, pqac-00000010, pqac-00000011, pqac-00000012, pqac-00000013, pqac-00000014, pqac-00000015, pqac-00000016, pqac-00000017, pqac-00000018, pqac-00000019, pqac-00000020, pqac-00000021, pqac-00000022, pqac-00000023, pqac-00000024, pqac-00000025, pqac-00000026, pqac-00000027, pqac-00000028, pqac-00000029, pqac-00000030, pqac-00000031, pqac-00000032, pqac-00000033]

Status: Paper Count=69 | Relevant Papers=11 | Clinical Trial Count=0 | Relevant Clinical Trials=0 | Current Evidence=34 | Current Cost=$0.2095 | Disease-Target Evidence=0

function.arguments: {"plan_objective_id": 5, "updated_plan_objective_json": "{\"id\":5,\"objective\":\"Synthesize findings into a structured conclusion about effectiveness, moderators (delivery mode, population, baseline severity), and limitations (risk of bias, heterogeneity, generalizability).\",\"rationale\":\"The user asks what the literature says; synthesis is needed beyond listing studies.\",\"status\":\"completed\",\"result\":\"Synthesis: (a) Symptom outcomes\u2014Across meta-analyses and RCTs, CBT-based interventions for work-related stress generally yield small-to-moderate average improvements, with some trials showing large effects on perceived stress/burnout (notably internet-based stress management programs). (b) Durability\u2014Benefits on stress/burnout commonly persist at least 6 months in iCBT/iSMI trials. (c) Work outcomes\u2014Evidence for absenteeism/RTW/work ability is weaker and inconsistent; pooled evidence in sick-listed samples shows small improvements in sick-leave days/RTW, but in occupational stress trials CBT often improves symptoms without changing absenteeism/work ability unless work-focused/RTW elements are included. (d) Moderators\u2014Greater work-outcome effects appear when CBT is explicitly work-focused (integrates work ability, workplace contact, problem-solving for work issues), and when baseline distress is clinically significant; guided formats can have similar or somewhat larger effects than unguided. (e) Limitations\u2014High heterogeneity, varied comparators and outcome measures, limited objective work-outcome reporting, and attrition in digital trials constrain certainty and generalizability.\",\"evaluation\":\"Objective met: integrates review-level and RCT evidence into coherent conclusions, including moderators and limitations aligned to workplace stress effectiveness question.\"}"}

(4) Asplund et al. 2023 employees with stress-related disorders: work-focused iCBT and generic iCBT both reduced burnout strongly (post d=1.00 and 0.83; 6-month d=0.74), and only work-focused iCBT improved work ability and reduced short-term sickness absence (hundreds of days fewer). (5) Salomonsson et al. 2020 sick-listed stress subgroup: CBT reduced symptoms more than RTW intervention posttreatment but did not reduce sick leave relative to RTW intervention.",

(b) Durability—Benefits on stress/burnout commonly persist at least 6 months in iCBT/iSMI trials. info.name: paper_search
info.description:
Search for papers in Google Scholar for you to look at.

Can be called repeatedly with identical parameters to retrieve more results (max 2 repeats).
A short summary of the retrieved papers will be returned, but to examine the full content of each paper,
you must use the gather_evidence tool.

query.description:
A Google Scholar search query, which can be a specific phrase, complete sentence,
or general keywords, e.g. 'machine learning for immunology'. Also can be
given search operators like 'author:'.
query.title: Query

min_year.description:
filter for minimum publication year.
The current year is 2026. If unsure about time range, do not set.
min_year.title: Min Year

max_year.description:
filter for maximum publication year.
The current year is 2026. If unsure about time range, do not set.
max_year.title: Max Year

info.name: update_plan
info.description:
Update the plan for a specific or new objective using a JSON string to create or
replace the existing objective.

The updated_json should be a JSON string representing a single plan objective object.

You may use this tool to update an objective, create a new one, or change
strategy if the current plan is not working. Only change strategy significantly
as a last resort, after trying very hard to make the current plan work.

Only mark an objective as completed if you are absolutely sure that it has been fully
met. If it is only partially met, you may add additional objectives to the plan for
what is missing.

plan_objective_id.description: The id of the objective to update.
plan_objective_id.title: Plan Objective Id

updated_plan_objective_json.description:
A JSON string representing the updated objective.
It should be a dictionary with the with the following keys:
- "id": (int) an integer id matching plan_objective_id, if not found, a new objective is created.
- "objective": (str) a human readable description of the objective's goal
- "rationale": (str) a human readable rationale
    describing why this objective is necessary and appropriate for the query.
- "status": (str) updated task status, default to pending,
    one of: pending, in-progress, or completed.
    "pending" is yet to be started,
    "in-progress" is the current objective being solved,
    "completed" is successfully completed with acceptance criteria met.
- "result": (str|None) The result of the objective, default to None.
- "evaluation": (str|None) Description of why the objective result
    meets the criteria for being complete. Default to None.
updated_plan_objective_json.title: Updated Plan Objective Json

info.name: create_artifact
info.description:
Create an artifact that can be used in the final answer, based on information
you have collected so far.

How it works: the full message history thus far is passed to another LLM,
along with the question and your description. The LLM is tasked to create
an artifact that matches your specifications, including a title, caption,
and markdown content. You drive the process by specifying the type and
describing what you want.

Only use this tool *after* you have gathered sufficient evidence or
information to create a new artifact. Do not call this tool in parallel
with tools that are searching for information or evidence that would be
necessary to create this artifact.

It is very helpful after calls to several search tools,
after calls to the `gather_evidence` tool, but it is not required.
However, it should be called before the `answer` tool at
least one time, but more may be given if specifically helpful for
the task, so that the `answer` tool can use the artifacts for the answer.

An artifact can be any string representable fact or list of facts,
formatted using markdown syntax. The artifact will be generated as one of
the following types:
- table: Markdown tables with rows and columns (e.g., findings, methods, molecules)
- code_block: Code snippets in fenced code blocks
- blockquote: Quoted text or important facts (using > prefix)
- image: AI-generated image (chart, diagram, or visualization)

Each artifact consists of:
- A short title (3-8 words) that names what the artifact contains
- A caption (1-2 sentences) describing what the artifact shows and why it's useful
- Markdown content in a single element type (validated automatically with retries if needed)
  For image type, content is a markdown image embed ``![title](data:image/png;base64,...)``,
  embedding the PNG directly as a base64 data URL.

artifact_type.description:
Artifact type to create. Must be one of: table,
code_block, blockquote, image.
artifact_type.title: Artifact Type

description.description:
Description of what you want in the artifact. Be specific
about what information should be included, how it should be organized,
and what columns/sections/content you want. This guides the summary LLM.
For image type, describe the visual you want generated in detail.
description.title: Description

info.name: open_targets_search
info.description:
Search for disease-target associations from OpenTargets database.

Adds new disease-target association data to the state and returns top search results.

disease_names.description:
List of disease names to search for.
Usage:
disease_names = ["Alzheimer's"] ->
    Returns top targets for Alzheimer's
disease_names = ["Alzheimer's", "Diabetes"], target_names = ["PSEN1"] ->
    Filters for only PSEN1-Alzheimer's/Diabetes evidence
disease_names = ["Alzheimer's"], drug_names = ["Aducanumab"] ->
    Filters for Alzheimer's disease-target pairs treated by Aducanumab
disease_names.title: Disease Names

target_names.description:
List of target names to search for.
Usage:
target_names = ["BACE1"] ->
    Returns top diseases associated with BACE1
target_names = ["BACE1", "APP"], disease_names = ["Alzheimer's"] ->
    Filters for only these target-Alzheimer's pairs
target_names = ["BACE1"], drug_names = ["Aducanumab"] ->
    Filters for BACE1 disease pairs treated by Aducanumab
target_names.title: Target Names

drug_names.description:
List of drug names to generate or filter results.
Usage:
drug_names = ["Aducanumab"] ->
    Returns all disease-target pairs treated by Aducanumab
drug_names = ["Aducanumab"], disease_names = ["Alzheimer's"] ->
    Filters for Alzheimer's pairs treated by Aducanumab
drug_names = ["Aducanumab"], target_names = ["BACE1"] ->
    Filters for BACE1 disease pairs treated by Aducanumab
drug_names.title: Drug Names

info.name: view_images
info.description:
Retrieve images from a document.

Use this tool when you need to see figures, tables, or other visual content.
The tool works by asking a weaker LLM agent to scan through the document, looking for
visual content matching the query you provide. When the agent finds matches, it will
crop the page to the relevant region. It can be tasked to return multiple images,
if desired. Note that the weaker LLM may be worse at *understanding* images than
you, so double-check its conclusions about the returned images. It is also stateless,
i.e. it will not remember what you have asked it to find in the past.

Use this tool carefully. It is difficult to retrieve images, and the images
themselves will fill up your context window quickly. Here are some guidelines:
1. ALWAYS use this tool if the question explicitly mentions 'Table X' or 'Figure Y'.
   These questions require visual content and cannot be answered with text alone.
2. Use this tool as a fallback when gather_evidence fails repeatedly (2+ times
   returning 'No relevant evidence'). This suggests the answer may be in visual content.
3. When gather_evidence returns text mentioning 'see Figure X' or 'shown in Table Y',
   you should call this tool to examine that specific visual reference.
4. Only call this tool after directly inspecting relevant text with read_text
   to confirm the document likely contains the visual you need.
5. Don't use this tool in a broad-spectrum way (don't call on many documents
   just to see what might be relevant).
6. Never call this tool in parallel with itself.
7. Users like visual evidence, so call this tool at least once before answering so you
   can provide some image citations in the answer. Add this to your plan so you do not forget.
   For images to be presented to the user, they must be cited in the final answer after retrieval
   by this tool. You may ignore this instruction if the request is for the question is asking
   for a specific piece of information and is not an open-ended research question.
8. NEVER cite full-page images in your final answer, unless they are full-page panels figures or
   tables. Cite relevant cropped regions if available, otherwise consider calling this tool again
   to find a smaller region that answers the query.

query.description:
Query to search for visual content in the document. If you know the specific
figure/table/formula you are looking for, you can specify it here (e.g. "find me
Figure 3" or "extract the table on page 10"). You can also request based on content
(e.g. "find me all volcano plots of gene X" or "I need the formula for the growth rate").
You can even include explicit instructions for the LLM agent to follow (if helpful, not
necessarily required). The more specific and clear your query is here, the higher the
chance of success.
query.title: Query

text_name.description: Name of a text chunk (e.g., 'smith2023example pages 1-5').
text_name.title: Text Name

info.name: gather_evidence
info.description:
Given a specific question, gather evidence from full-text of documents that have been retrieved by
previous tool calls.

Here is how the tool works: the provided question is posed to all texts retrieved thus far
by other tools. An LLM sees each text independently and is prompted to find information relevant
to the question.

That is, we sample evidence `e \sim LLM(prompt, text, q)` for each text. We only return pieces of
evidence `e` that are sufficiently relevant to the question; if the text is irrelevant, then `e`
is not included in the final response of this tool. Note that this LLM does not see the conversation
history thus far, only the question and each text. Note that it only sees text, no images or other
media from the contents (though tables and formulae may be parseable in some cases).

Think of this tool as an efficient way to deeply scan all retrieved documents for evidence. It
is complementary to the `read_text` tool, which can be used to directly inspect a subset of the
retrieved documents.

A valuable time to invoke this tool is after another tool finds potentially relevant documents.
Feel free to invoke this tool in parallel with other tools, but do not call this tool in parallel with itself.
Only invoke this tool when the document count is above zero, or this tool will be useless.

If you call this tool 2+ times and repeatedly get 'No relevant evidence found',
consider whether the answer might be in visual content (figures/tables) rather than text,
and use appropriate tools if available to access that content.

Once you receive evidence from this tool:

1. Read Carefully: Don't skim. The evidence may contain multiple similar terms
   or values—make sure you identify the RIGHT one.

2. For Comparative Questions: If the question asks "which X has greatest Y",
   check that the evidence includes information about ALL relevant X options, not
   just one or two. If you only see partial information, gather more evidence.

3. Check for Noise: Verify that numbers or terms aren't just metadata
   (page numbers, reference IDs, dates). Look for the value in meaningful
   scientific context.

4. Context Matters: A term appearing 71 times doesn't mean it's always the answer.
   Verify which occurrence actually answers the question asked.

If the evidence doesn't clearly answer the question, gather more evidence before
concluding. Don't guess based on partial information.

question.description: Specific question to gather evidence for.
question.title: Question

text_name_focus_list.description:
if there are known text names that are relevant to the question,
you can specify them here. This will ensure that the tool will look at these text names
first, and then look at the rest of the evidence. If left as `None`, an embedding
search will be performed on underlying content to find most relevant texts.
text_name_focus_list.title: Text Name Focus List

info.name: patent_search
info.description:
Search for patents to increase the patent count.

Repeat previous calls with the same query to continue a search.
Only repeat a maximum of three times.
This tool can be called concurrently.
This tool introduces novel patents, so invoke it when just beginning
or when unsatisfied with the current evidence.

query.description:
A search query for patents (using Google Patents). Never include temporal
filters here (e.g. specific years or dates); instead use the `after` and `before`
arguments. Never include author filters here; instead use the `inventor` argument.
Supports the following syntax, but they can be brittle and are not preferred; try
simple queries where possible:
- Boolean operators: AND (default), OR, and - (NOT)
- Exact phrases: "quoted text"
- Proximity: NEAR/x, ADJ/x (ordered), WITH (20 words), SAME (200 words)
- Field search: TI=(title), AB=(abstract), CL=(claims)
- CPC codes: cpc:A01B or CPC=B60R22/low (includes children)
- Wildcards: ? (0-1 char), * (0+ chars), # (exactly 1 char)
- Chemistry: exact molecule names, SSS=molecule (substructure),
  ~molecule (similar), SMILES strings, SMARTS=pattern, InChI keys,
  CL=~molecule (chemistry in claims)
query.title: Query

after.description:
Minimum patent date. Format: YYYYMMDD.
Example: '20150101' for patents after Jan 1, 2015.
after.title: After

before.description:
Maximum patent date. Format: YYYYMMDD.
Example: '20200101' for patents before Jan 1, 2020.
before.title: Before

date_type.description:
Type of date that after and before refer to. Must be either 'publication'
or 'filing'. Only necessary to set if after and/or before are set.
date_type.title: Date Type

inventor.description: Filter by inventor name(s). Comma-separated for multiple.
inventor.title: Inventor

info.name: clinical_trials_search
info.description:
Search for clinical trials, with support for repeated calls and concurrent execution.

Will add new clinical trials to the state, and return metadata about the number of trials found.

query.description:
The search query string. Supports complex boolean expressions, field-specific
searches, and query modifiers through operators. All configuration is done through
operators in the query string.
Query Syntax:
    Basic Search:
        Simple text automatically uses default EXPANSION[Relaxation] and COVERAGE[Contains]
        >>> "heart attack"

    Modified Search:
        Use operators to modify search behavior:
        >>> 'EXPANSION[None]COVERAGE[FullMatch]"exact phrase"'
        >>> 'EXPANSION[Concept]heart attack'

    Field Search:
        Specify fields using AREA operator:
        >>> 'AREA[InterventionName]aspirin'
        >>> 'AREA[Phase]PHASE3'

    Location Search:
        Use SEARCH operator for compound location queries:
        >>> 'cancer AND SEARCH[Location](AREA[LocationCity]Boston AND AREA[LocationState]Massachusetts)'

    Complex Boolean:
        Combine terms with AND, OR, NOT and parentheses:
        >>> '(cancer OR tumor) AND NOT (EXPANSION[None]pediatric OR AREA[StdAge]CHILD)'

    Date Ranges:
        Use RANGE to specify date ranges with formats like "yyyy-MM" or "yyyy-MM-dd".
        Note that MIN and MAX can be used for open-ended ranges:
        >>> AREA[ResultsFirstPostDate]RANGE[2015-01-01, MAX]

Operators:
    EXPANSION[type]: Controls term expansion
        - None: Exact match only, case and accent sensitive
        - Term: Includes lexical variants (plurals, spellings)
        - Concept: Includes UMLS synonyms
        - Relaxation: Relaxes adjacency requirements (default)
        - Lossy: Allows missing partial terms

    COVERAGE[type]: Controls text matching
        - FullMatch: Must match entire field
        - StartsWith: Must match beginning of field
        - EndsWith: Must match end of field
        - Contains: Must match part of field (default)

    AREA[field]: Specifies field to search
        - See Field Reference for available fields

    SEARCH[type]: Groups field searches
        - Location: Groups location-related fields
        - Study: Groups study-related fields

Usage Notes:
    - All search expressions are implicitly OR expressions
    - Operator precedence (highest to lowest): terms/source operators, NOT/context operators, AND, OR
    - Use quotes for exact phrase matching: "heart attack"
    - Use parentheses for grouping: (heart OR cardiac) AND attack
    - Use backslash to escape operators: \AND
    - Default expansion is EXPANSION[Relaxation]
    - Default coverage is COVERAGE[Contains]

Field Reference:
    High Priority Fields (weight >= 0.8):
        - NCTId (1.0): Trial identifier
        - Acronym (1.0): Study acronym
        - BriefTitle (0.89): Short title
        - OfficialTitle (0.85): Full official title
        - Condition (0.81): Medical condition
        - InterventionName (0.8): Primary intervention name
        - OverallStatus: Trial status

    Medium Priority Fields (0.5-0.79):
        - InterventionOtherName (0.75): Alternative intervention names
        - Phase (0.65): Trial phase
        - StdAge (0.65): Standard age groups
        - Keyword (0.6): Study keywords
        - BriefSummary (0.6): Short description
        - SecondaryOutcomeMeasure (0.5): Secondary outcomes

    Low Priority Fields (< 0.5):
        - DesignPrimaryPurpose (0.3): Primary purpose of study
        - StudyType (0.3)
        - Various descriptive, location, and administrative fields

Supported Enums:
    Phase:
        - EARLY_PHASE1: Early Phase 1
        - PHASE1: Phase 1
        - PHASE2: Phase 2
        - PHASE3: Phase 3
        - PHASE4: Phase 4
        - NA: Not Applicable

    StandardAge:
        - CHILD: Child
        - ADULT: Adult
        - OLDER_ADULT: Older Adult

    Status:
        - RECRUITING: Currently recruiting participants
        - ACTIVE_NOT_RECRUITING: Active but not recruiting
        - COMPLETED: Study completed
        - ENROLLING_BY_INVITATION: Enrolling by invitation only
        - NOT_YET_RECRUITING: Not yet recruiting
        - SUSPENDED: Study suspended
        - TERMINATED: Study terminated
        - WITHDRAWN: Study withdrawn
        - AVAILABLE: Available
        - NO_LONGER_AVAILABLE: No longer available
        - TEMPORARILY_NOT_AVAILABLE: Temporarily not available
        - APPROVED_FOR_MARKETING: Approved for marketing
        - WITHHELD: Withheld
        - UNKNOWN: Unknown status

    StudyType:
        - INTERVENTIONAL: Interventional studies
        - OBSERVATIONAL: Observational studies
        - EXPANDED_ACCESS: Expanded access studies

    PrimaryPurpose:
        - TREATMENT: Treatment
        - PREVENTION: Prevention
        - DIAGNOSTIC: Diagnostic
        - ECT: Educational/Counseling/Training
        - SUPPORTIVE_CARE: Supportive Care
        - SCREENING: Screening
        - HEALTH_SERVICES_RESEARCH: Health Services Research
        - BASIC_SCIENCE: Basic Science
        - DEVICE_FEASIBILITY: Device Feasibility
        - OTHER: Other

    InterventionType:
        - BEHAVIORAL: Behavioral interventions
        - BIOLOGICAL: Biological interventions
        - COMBINATION_PRODUCT: Combination product interventions
        - DEVICE: Device interventions
        - DIAGNOSTIC_TEST: Diagnostic test interventions
        - DIETARY_SUPPLEMENT: Dietary supplement interventions
        - DRUG: Drug interventions
        - GENETIC: Genetic interventions
        - PROCEDURE: Procedure interventions
        - RADIATION: Radiation interventions
        - OTHER: Other interventions

    DesignAllocation:
        - RANDOMIZED: Randomized allocation
        - NON_RANDOMIZED: Non-randomized allocation
        - NA: Not applicable

    InterventionalAssignment:
        - SINGLE_GROUP: Single group assignment
        - PARALLEL: Parallel assignment
        - CROSSOVER: Crossover assignment
        - FACTORIAL: Factorial assignment
        - SEQUENTIAL: Sequential assignment

    ObservationalModel:
        - COHORT: Cohort
        - CASE_CONTROL: Case-Control
        - CASE_ONLY: Case-Only
        - CASE_CROSSOVER: Case-Crossover
        - ECOLOGIC_OR_COMMUNITY: Ecologic or Community
        - FAMILY_BASED: Family-Based
        - DEFINED_POPULATION: Defined Population
        - NATURAL_HISTORY: Natural History
        - OTHER: Other

    DesignMasking:
        - NONE: None (Open Label)
        - SINGLE: Single
        - DOUBLE: Double
        - TRIPLE: Triple
        - QUADRUPLE: Quadruple

    WhoMasked:
        - PARTICIPANT: Participant
        - CARE_PROVIDER: Care Provider
        - INVESTIGATOR: Investigator
        - OUTCOMES_ASSESSOR: Outcomes Assessor
query.title: Query

info.name: read_text
info.description:
Given a list of text names, read their contents.

This tool is valuable to read the full contents of specific parts of documents,
complementing the `gather_evidence` tool's indirect approach.
This can be an especially useful tool if `gather_evidence` does not find evidence,
but you think it is very likely that specific documents are still relevant to the question.

text_name_list.description:
A list of up to 10 text names to read.
These text names can be any valid text names retrieved by other tools.
text_name_list.title: Text Name List

info.name: create_plan
info.description:
Create a plan for to construct a thorough and accurate answer.

Decompose the input task into a set of sub-objectives that need to
be met in order to form the answer. Subsequently, when working on solving
the query, you can focus on these objectives one by one. Note that the
`update_plan` tool allows you to change the objectives if needed.

In addition to the objectives you deem necessary, add one final objective
to call the `answer` tool with your final answer, so that you do not
forget to submit your answer.

plan_json.description:
A JSON string representing the plan. It should be a dictionary
with top-level key "objectives", whose value is a list of objectives.
Each objective should be a dictionary with the following keys:
- "id": (int) an integer id for the plan objective
- "objective": (str) a human readable description of the objective's goal
- "rationale": (str) a human readable rationale
    describing why this objective is necessary and appropriate for the query.
- "status": (str) updated task status, default to pending,
    one of: pending, in-progress, or completed.
    "pending" is yet to be started,
    "in-progress" is the current objective being solved,
    "completed" is successfully completed with acceptance criteria met.
- "result": (str|None) The result of the objective, default to None.
- "evaluation": (str|None) Description of why the objective result
    meets the criteria for being complete. Default to None.
plan_json.title: Plan Json

info.name: collect_cited_papers_in_evidence
info.description:
Collect papers by traversing the citations of relevant papers to increase the paper count.

This tool has no effect if called when paper count or relevant papers are zero.
This tool will find papers that are likely to lead to more relevant evidence.
The papers found have a higher chance of relevance because this tool starts from relevant evidence.
This tool's usefulness increases with the amount of gathered evidence as there's more citations to consider.

info.name: answer
info.description:
Submit your final answer to the user. You MUST call this tool on your final answer.
If you mark all objectives as completed but have not yet called this tool, then
the answer will not be submitted. Note that this tool should only be called once, as it
immediately terminates the run and submits the answer.

Write an answer based on the evidence from gather_evidence results in message history.
If the context provides insufficient information, provide a partial answer and explain
why it is partial (e.g. the question is currently an open question in the field,
you were unable to obtain certain papers, etc.).

For each part of your answer, indicate which sources most support it via citation IDs at
the end of sentences, like (pqac-0000ABCD).
You are only allowed to cite available context IDs, indicated in previous messages by "Context IDs that may be cited:".
## Valid citation examples, only use comma/space delimited parentheticals:

- (pqac-0000ABCD, pqac-0000EFGH)
- (pqac-0000IJKL)

## Invalid citation examples:

- (pqac-0000ABCD and pqac-0000EFGH)
- (pqac-0000ABCD;pqac-0000EFGH)
- (pqac-0000ABCD-pqac-0000EFGH)
- pqac-0000ABCD and pqac-0000EFGH
- Example's work pqac-0000ABCD
- (pages pqac-0000ABCD)
- ![description](pqac-0000ABCD)
- ()

Do not concatenate citation ids, just use them as-is.

When writing your answer, embed artifacts if relevant artifacts have been previously
created by the `create_artifact` tool. To embed an artifact, simply include this in your
answer, on its own line and without any additional formatting (i.e. no backticks, code fences,
quotes, additional tags, etc.).
Always provide at least some introductory sentence or paragraph before referring to an artifact,
so that the artifact is properly contextualized.
Here is an example reference to an artifact with ID "artifact-XY":
<artifact id="artifact-XY"/>

Valid artifact IDs are indicated in previous messages by 'Artifact IDs that may be injected into the answer:'.


Double-check your answer:

1. Evidence Match: Re-read the specific evidence snippets you're citing. Does your
   answer actually match what they say? Don't rely on memory from 20+ messages ago.

2. For Comparative Questions ("which X has greatest/maximum/highest Y"):
   - Did you examine ALL options mentioned in the evidence?
   - Did you explicitly compare their values?
   - Example: If evidence mentions "Pancreatic: 0.92, Liver: 0.85, Lung: 0.78",
     verify you compared all three, not just two.

3. For Specific Value Questions ("How many", "What is the", specific terms):
   - Does the exact value you're stating appear in the evidence?
   - Are you extracting from the right context (not noise like page numbers)?

4. Sanity Check: Read your answer. Does it directly address what was asked?

Don't use Markdown image syntax like ![...](...) to reference media in your answer,
because media get automatically linked by the system based on your answer's citations.

Write in the style of a scientific article, with concise sentences and
coherent paragraphs. This answer will be used directly,
so do not add any extraneous information.

answer.description: valid answer, cited with citation IDs.
answer.title: Answer

messages.content:
Use the tools to answer the question: What does peer-reviewed literature say about the effectiveness of cognitive behavioural therapy for workplace stress?

Start by making a plan, and update the plan as you go. Your answer will be returned to the user when all plan objectives are completed. Be thorough and ensure your evidence matches the user's question.

function.arguments: {"plan_json": "{\"objectives\":[{\"id\":1,\"objective\":\"Define scope and key outcomes for CBT targeting workplace stress (e.g., perceived stress, burnout, anxiety/depression, absenteeism/work functioning) and inclusion criteria (peer-reviewed, working populations, CBT or CBT-based interventions).\",\"rationale\":\"Clarifies what counts as CBT for workplace stress and what \u2018effectiveness\u2019 means so evidence extraction is aligned with the user\u2019s question.\",\"status\":\"pending\",\"result\":null,\"evaluation\":null},{\"id\":2,\"objective\":\"Identify high-quality peer-reviewed syntheses (systematic reviews/meta-analyses) on CBT for work-related stress and related occupational outcomes.\",\"rationale\":\"Syntheses provide the most reliable estimate of effectiveness and typical effect sizes across studies.\",\"status\":\"pending\",\"result\":null,\"evaluation\":null},{\"id\":3,\"objective\":\"Identify and extract evidence from key randomized controlled trials of CBT delivered in workplace/occupational contexts (face-to-face, group, online/iCBT), including outcomes and follow-up durability.\",\"rationale\":\"RCTs provide direct causal evidence and allow characterization of intervention formats and magnitude/duration of benefit.\",\"status\":\"pending\",\"result\":null,\"evaluation\":null},{\"id\":4,\"objective\":\"Retrieve at least one relevant figure/table via image extraction to support quantitative results (effect sizes, forest plot, or key outcome table).\",\"rationale\":\"The tool guidance requests using view_images at least once; figures/tables often summarize effect sizes and enhance evidentiary support.\",\"status\":\"pending\",\"result\":null,\"evaluation\":null},{\"id\":5,\"objective\":\"Synthesize findings into a structured conclusion about effectiveness, moderators (delivery mode, population, baseline severity), and limitations (risk of bias, heterogeneity, generalizability).\",\"rationale\":\"The user asks what the literature says; synthesis is needed beyond listing studies.\",\"status\":\"pending\",\"result\":null,\"evaluation\":null},{\"id\":6,\"objective\":\"Create at least one artifact (e.g., summary table of key reviews/RCTs and effects) for inclusion in final answer.\",\"rationale\":\"Artifacts improve clarity and ensure the final response is a technical document with organized evidence.\",\"status\":\"pending\",\"result\":null,\"evaluation\":null},{\"id\":7,\"objective\":\"Draft and submit the final cited answer using the answer tool.\",\"rationale\":\"Required to deliver the final response to the user.\",\"status\":\"pending\",\"result\":null,\"evaluation\":null}]}"}
function.name: create_plan

messages.content:
Plan registered and formatted correctly, with some modifications:
{
  "objectives": [
    {
      "id": 1,
      "objective": "Define scope and key outcomes for CBT targeting workplace stress (e.g., perceived stress, burnout, anxiety/depression, absenteeism/work functioning) and inclusion criteria (peer-reviewed, working populations, CBT or CBT-based interventions).",
      "rationale": "Clarifies what counts as CBT for workplace stress and what ‘effectiveness’ means so evidence extraction is aligned with the user’s question.",
      "status": "pending",
      "result": null,
      "evaluation": null
    },
    {
      "id": 2,
      "objective": "Identify high-quality peer-reviewed syntheses (systematic reviews/meta-analyses) on CBT for work-related stress and related occupational outcomes.",
      "rationale": "Syntheses provide the most reliable estimate of effectiveness and typical effect sizes across studies.",
      "status": "pending",
      "result": null,
      "evaluation": null
    },
    {
      "id": 3,
      "objective": "Identify and extract evidence from key randomized controlled trials of CBT delivered in workplace/occupational contexts (face-to-face, group, online/iCBT), including outcomes and follow-up durability.",
      "rationale": "RCTs provide direct causal evidence and allow characterization of intervention formats and magnitude/duration of benefit.",
      "status": "pending",
      "result": null,
      "evaluation": null
    },
    {
      "id": 4,
      "objective": "Retrieve at least one relevant figure/table via image extraction to support quantitative results (effect sizes, forest plot, or key outcome table).",
      "rationale": "The tool guidance requests using view_images at least once; figures/tables often summarize effect sizes and enhance evidentiary support.",
      "status": "pending",
      "result": null,
      "evaluation": null
    },

Retrieved Papers:
## 1. A randomized controlled intervention of workplace-based group cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia
Valid Text Names:
'schiller2018arandomizedcontrolled pages 1-2', 'schiller2018arandomizedcontrolled pages 2-4', 'schiller2018arandomizedcontrolled pages 4-6', 'schiller2018arandomizedcontrolled pages 6-7', 'schiller2018arandomizedcontrolled pages 7-9', 'schiller2018arandomizedcontrolled pages 9-11', 'schiller2018arandomizedcontrolled pages 11-12', 'schiller2018arandomizedcontrolled pages 12-12'

BibTex:
@article{schiller2018arandomizedcontrolled,
    author = "Schiller, Helena and Söderström, Marie and Lekander, Mats and Rajaleid, Kristiina and Kecklund, Göran",
    title = "A randomized controlled intervention of workplace-based group cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia",
    year = "2018",
    journal = "International Archives of Occupational and Environmental Health",
    volume = "91",
    pages = "413-424",
    month = "Jan",
    doi = "10.1007/s00420-018-1291-x",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.1007/s00420-018-1291-x",
    publisher = "Springer Science and Business Media LLC",
    issue = "4",
    issn = "0340-0131"
}


Abstract:
PurposeSleep disturbance is common in the working population, often associated with work stress, health complaints and impaired work performance. This study evaluated a group intervention at work, based on cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) for insomnia, and the moderating effects of burnout scores at baseline.MethodsThis is a randomized controlled intervention with a waiting list control group. Participants were employees working at least 75% of full time, reporting self-perceived regular sleep problems. Data were collected at baseline, post-intervention and at a 3-month follow-up through diaries, wrist-actigraphy and questionnaires including the Insomnia Severity Index (ISI) and the Shirom–Melamed Burnout Questionnaire (SMBQ). Fifty-one participants (63% women) completed data collections.ResultsA multilevel mixed model showed no significant differences between groups for sleep over time, while there was a significant effect on insomnia symptoms when excluding participants working shifts (N = 11) from the analysis (p = 0.044). Moreover, a moderating effect of baseline-levels of burnout scores was observed on insomnia symptoms (p = 0.009). A post-hoc analysis showed that individuals in the intervention group with low burnout scores at baseline (SMBQ < 3.75) displayed significantly reduced ISI scores at follow-up, compared to individuals with high burnout scores at baseline (p = 0.005).ConclusionsGroup CBT for insomnia given at the workplace did not reduce sleep problems looking at the group as a whole, while it was indicated that the intervention reduced insomnia in employees with regular daytime work. The results also suggest that workplace-based group CBT may improve sleep in employees with primary insomnia if not concomitant with high burnout scores.

… work-related stress and sleep problems, this is to our knowledge the first randomized controlled study to … -intervention for insomnia in a working population, conducted at the workplace. …

Abstract:
<jats:sec><jats:title>Background</jats:title><jats:p>Little is known about the efficacy of educational interventions for reducing the stigma associated with depression.</jats:p></jats:sec><jats:sec><jats:title>Aims</jats:title><jats:p>To investigate the effects on stigma of two internet depression sites.</jats:p></jats:sec><jats:sec><jats:title>Method</jats:title><jats:p>A sample of 525 individuals with elevated scores on a depression assessment scale were randomly allocated to a depression information website (BluePages), a cognitive–behavioural skills training website (MoodGYM) or an attention control condition. Personal stigma (personal stigmatising attitudes to depression) and perceived stigma (perception of what most other people believe) were assessed before and after the intervention.</jats:p></jats:sec><jats:sec><jats:title>Results</jats:title><jats:p>Relative to the control, the internet sites significantly reduced personal stigma, although the effects were small. BluePages had no effect on perceived stigma and MoodGYM was associated with an increase in perceived stigma relative to the control. Changes in stigma were not mediated by changes in depression, depression literacy or cognitive–behavioural therapy literacy.</jats:p></jats:sec><jats:sec><jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title><jats:p>The internet warrants further investigation as a means of delivering stigma reduction programmes for depression.</jats:p></jats:sec>

## 10. Log in and breathe out: efficacy and cost-effectiveness of an online sleep training for teachers affected by work-related strain - study protocol for a randomized controlled trial
Valid Text Names:
'thiart2013loginand pages 1-2', 'thiart2013loginand pages 2-4', 'thiart2013loginand pages 4-5', 'thiart2013loginand pages 5-6', 'thiart2013loginand pages 6-8', 'thiart2013loginand pages 8-9', 'thiart2013loginand pages 9-10', 'thiart2013loginand pages 9-9', 'thiart2013loginand pages 10-10'

BibTex:
@article{thiart2013loginand,
    author = "Thiart, Hanne and Lehr, Dirk and Ebert, David and Sieland, Bernhard and Berking, Matthias and Riper, Heleen",
    title = "Log in and breathe out: efficacy and cost-effectiveness of an online sleep training for teachers affected by work-related strain - study protocol for a randomized controlled trial",
    year = "2013",
    journal = "Trials",
    volume = "14",
    pages = "169-169",
    month = "Jun",
    doi = "10.1186/1745-6215-14-169",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.1186/1745-6215-14-169",
    publisher = "Springer Science and Business Media LLC",
    issue = "1",
    issn = "1745-6215"
}


Abstract:
BackgroundInsomnia and work-related stress often co-occur. Both are associated with personal distress and diminished general functioning, as well as substantial socio-economic costs due to, for example, reduced productivity at the work place and absenteeism. Insomnia complaints by people experiencing work-related stress are correlated with a deficient cognitive detachment from work. Diffuse boundaries between work and private life can additionally complicate the use of recreational activities that facilitate cognitive detachment.Cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia is effective but rarely implemented. Internet-based cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia could potentially reduce this deficit given its demonstrated effectiveness. Less is known, however, about the efficacy of internet-based cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia in populations affected by high work stress. Thus, the aim of the present study is to evaluate the efficacy and cost-effectiveness of a newly developed, guided online training which is based on Cognitive Behavioral Therapy for insomnia and tailored to teachers affected by occupational stress.Methods/DesignIn a two-arm randomized controlled trial (N = 128), the effects of a guided online sleep training will be compared to a waitlist-control condition. German teachers with significant clinical insomnia complaints (Insomnia Severity Index ≥15) and work-related rumination (Irritation Scale, subscale Cognitive Irritation ≥15) will be included in the study. The primary outcome measure will be insomnia severity. Additionally, an economic evaluation from a societal perspective will be conducted. Data from the intention-to-treat sample will be analyzed two and six months after randomization.DiscussionTo the best of our knowledge, this is the first study to evaluate an online sleep training tailored to a specific population with work stress, that is, teachers. If this type of intervention is effective, it could reduce the paucity of cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia and augment the support for teachers in coping with their insomnia problems.Trial registrationGerman Clinical Trial Register (DRKS):DRKS00004700

## 1. Effect of stress management based on cognitive–behavioural therapy on nurses as a universal prevention in the workplace: a systematic review and meta-analysis protocol
Valid Text Names:
'kuribayashi2022effectofstress pages 1-2', 'kuribayashi2022effectofstress pages 2-3', 'kuribayashi2022effectofstress pages 3-4', 'kuribayashi2022effectofstress pages 4-5', 'kuribayashi2022effectofstress pages 5-5'

BibTex:
@article{kuribayashi2022effectofstress,
    author = "Kuribayashi, Kazuto and Takano, Ayumi and Inagaki, Akiko and Imamura, Kotaro and Kawakami, Norito",
    title = "Effect of stress management based on cognitive–behavioural therapy on nurses as a universal prevention in the workplace: a systematic review and meta-analysis protocol",
    year = "2022",
    journal = "BMJ Open",
    volume = "12",
    pages = "e062516",
    month = "Sep",
    doi = "10.1136/bmjopen-2022-062516",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.1136/bmjopen-2022-062516",
    publisher = "BMJ",
    issue = "9",
    issn = "2044-6055"
}


Abstract:
<jats:sec><jats:title>Introduction</jats:title><jats:p>The mental health status of nurses affects not only their well-being but also the organisational outcomes and the quality of patient care. Hence, stress management strategies are critical as a universal prevention measure that address an entire population and are not directed at a specific risk group to maintain nurses’ mental health in the workplace. No systematic review or meta-analysis has been conducted to evaluate the effect of cognitive–behavioural therapy (CBT) that specifically focuses on universal prevention. Therefore, the aim of this study is to examine the effectiveness that is reported in published randomised controlled trial (RCT) studies.</jats:p></jats:sec><jats:sec><jats:title>Methods and analysis</jats:title><jats:p>This systematic review and meta-analysis will analyse published studies selected from electronic databases (ie, Cochrane Central Register of Controlled Trials, PubMed, Cumulative Index to Nursing and Allied Health Literature, PsycINFO, PsycARTICLES, Web of Science and the Japan Medical Abstracts Society). The inclusion criteria for studies are that they (1) were conducted to assess the effect of CBT on the mental health of nurses as a universal prevention, (2) used an RCT design and (3) provided sufficient results (sample sizes, means and SD) to estimate the pooled effect sizes with 95% CIs. Studies will be excluded if they only targeted nurses who had been screened as being at high risk in terms of their mental health and indicated that they required the prevention. The methodological quality of the included studies will be assessed using the Cochrane Collaboration’s risk of bias tool.</jats:p></jats:sec><jats:sec><jats:title>Ethics and dissemination</jats:title><jats:p>Ethical approval is not required because this study is based on information obtained from previous studies. The results and findings of this study will be submitted for publication in a peer-reviewed international scientific journal. Results from this study will be helpful when implementing CBT strategies for nurses as a universal preventative measure in the workplace and for managing stress-related outcomes.</jats:p></jats:sec><jats:sec><jats:title>PROSPERO registration number</jats:title><jats:p>CRD42020152837.</jats:p></jats:sec>

Abstract:
Purpose Previous research has systematically studied the effectiveness of Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT)-based interventions in managing both mental and physical symptoms of chronic disease including depression, stress-related mental disorders (SMD), and chronic pain that are common causes of sick leave. However, a systematic review focusing on the effectiveness of CBT in facilitating RTW is lacking. This study compiles research on utilizing CBT-based interventions for helping employees on sick leave return to work. Methods Randomized controlled trials (RCT) published between 1 January 1990 and 27 June 2022 were searched in MEDLINE, EMBASE, The Cochrane Library, Scopus, PsycINFO, Web of Science, and PubMed. The primary outcome variables included a return to work (RTW) measure and sickness absences. The secondary outcomes include psychological conditions (mental illness, stress, anxiety, and depression) and physical condition (working ability, fatigue, and physical function). Results Thirty-four RCTs were included in the analysis. Fifteen RCTs with 1727 participants reported on sick leave. Results showed that participants who completed CBT intervention had reduced sick leave in days (mean reduction − 3.654; 95%CI − 5.253, − 2.046; p < 0.001) compared to the control group. Sixteen papers with 2298 participants reported that the intervention group RTW 1.5 days earlier (95%CI 1.019, 1.722; p < 0.05). CBT-based interventions were effective in managing fatigue, mental illness, and depression, and improving physical function while it showed no effects in managing stress, anxiety and working ability. Conclusions The findings indicate that CBT-based interventions are effective in reducing the length of sick leave and facilitating the RTW of employees in the intervention group.

Abstract:
The workplace represents a relevant source of stress for workers, being a risk factor for many mental disorders and psychological difficulties, including burn-out syndrome. Healthcare workers and other help-professions are particularly susceptible to work-related stress. The present systematic review aims to (1) identify available interventions for managing workplace-related stress symptoms; (2) assess their efficacy; and (3) discuss the current limitations of available interventions. A systematic review has been conducted, searching on PubMed, APA PsycInfo, and Scopus databases. Eighteen papers have been identified, which included different interventions for the management of work-related stress in healthcare professionals. These approaches can be grouped as follows: (1) interventions focusing on the individual level using cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) approaches; (2) interventions focusing on the individual level using relaxation techniques; and (3) interventions focusing on the organizational level. As regards interventions targeting the individual level using CBT approaches, mindfulness-based interventions were effective in reducing levels of burn-out, stress, and anxiety and in improving quality of life. As regards intervention using relaxation techniques, including art therapy, Emotional Freedom Techniques (ECT) and brief resilience retreats had a positive effect on the levels of anxiety, stress, and burnout. As regards interventions at the organizational level, we found no evidence for supporting its effectiveness in reducing the levels of burnout. Furthermore, available studies are heterogeneous in terms of assessment tools, target populations, and type of interventions, which limits the generalizability of findings.

## 4. A systematic review and meta-analysis of the effectiveness of individual-level interventions to reduce occupational stress perceptions among teachers

    publisher = "Informa UK Limited",
    issue = "7",
    issn = "1933-8244"
}


Abstract:
Abstract Teachers are largely affected by OS, during their most productive years, leading to catastrophic physical, psychological, and economic burden on themselves, families and society by large. Teachers are responsible for producing the workforce of any nation, and thus have a major impact indirectly on the economy and health of a nation. There are several individual-level interventions conducted to reduce occupational stress (OS) among teachers. This study was conducted to systematically review and conduct a meta-analysis of the effectiveness of individual-level interventions to reduce occupational stress among teachers. This review was registered in the International Prospective Register of Systematic Reviews (PROSPERO; Registration ID: CRD42020149277) and followed Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) guidelines. PubMed, Scopus, Cochrane, PsycInfo, and Web of Science databases were searched using predefined search strategies covering the eligibility criteria. Studies were selected in four rounds. Risk of bias assessments were conducted with GRADE recommendations and ROBINS-I criteria for randomized and non-randomized interventions, respectively. Study selection, data extraction and bias assessments were performed independ ently by two reviewers with a third reviewer to resolve conflicts. Narrative synthesis of the findings were also performed. Clinical, methodological, and statistical heterogeneity assessments were conduct ed. Meta-analyses were performed with Review Manager 5.3 software using the "generic inverse variance method" with mean difference as the pooled estimate. Sensitivity analyses of the findings was also performed. At the initial search, 293 articles were identified and 29 subsequently selected for synthesis of findings. The findings of the meta-analysis indicated that Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT) and relaxation interventions significantly reduced OS among teachers with an effect size of 6.2 (p < 0.001). Effect size was highest for combined CBT and relaxation interventions which was 6.57 (p < 0.001). Relaxation interventions only and CBT interventions only had effect sizes of 3.35 (p < 0.001) and 3.12 (p = 0.002) respectively. In sensitivity analysis, after removing low-quality studies, CBT interventions only, combined CBT and relaxation interventions and overall effect size were 6.31 (p < 0.00001), 17.36 (p < 0.00001), and 14.55 (p < 0.00001), respectively, ie, substantially greater than with low-quality studies included. Inconclusion, CBT and relaxation interventions reduce OS among teachers and it's most effective when those individual-level interventions are conducted together.

## 7. Work-focused cognitive behavioral intervention for psychological complaints in patients on sick leave due to work-related stress: Results from a randomized controlled trial

    title = "Work-focused cognitive behavioral intervention for psychological complaints in patients on sick leave due to work-related stress: Results from a randomized controlled trial",

    journal = "Journal of Negative Results in Biomedicine",
