Abstract:
BACKGROUND
Work disability such as sickness absence is common in people with depression.


OBJECTIVES
To evaluate the effectiveness of interventions aimed at reducing work disability in employees with depressive disorders.


SEARCH METHODS
We searched CENTRAL (The Cochrane Library), MEDLINE, Embase, CINAHL, and PsycINFO until April 4th 2020.


SELECTION CRITERIA
We included randomised controlled trials (RCTs) and cluster-RCTs of work-directed and clinical interventions for depressed people that included days of sickness absence or being off work as an outcome. We also analysed the effects on depression and work functioning.


DATA COLLECTION AND ANALYSIS
Two review authors independently extracted the data and rated the certainty of the evidence using GRADE. We used standardised mean differences (SMDs) or risk ratios (RR) with 95% confidence intervals (CI) to pool study results in studies we judged to be sufficiently similar.  MAIN RESULTS: In this update, we added 23 new studies. In total, we included 45 studies with 88 study arms, involving 12,109 participants with either a major depressive disorder or a high level of depressive symptoms. Risk of bias The most common types of bias risk were detection bias (27 studies) and attrition bias (22 studies), both for the outcome of sickness absence. Work-directed interventions Work-directed interventions combined with clinical interventions A combination of a work-directed intervention and a clinical intervention probably reduces days of sickness absence within the first year of follow-up (SMD -0.25, 95% CI -0.38 to -0.12; 9 studies; moderate-certainty evidence). This translates back to 0.5 fewer (95% CI -0.7 to -0.2) sick leave days in the past two weeks or 25 fewer days during one year (95% CI -37.5 to -11.8). The intervention does not lead to fewer persons being off work beyond one year follow-up (RR 0.96, 95% CI 0.85 to 1.09; 2 studies, high-certainty evidence). The intervention may reduce depressive symptoms (SMD -0.25, 95% CI -0.49 to -0.01; 8 studies, low-certainty evidence) and probably has a small effect on work functioning (SMD -0.19, 95% CI -0.42 to 0.06; 5 studies, moderate-certainty evidence) within the first year of follow-up.  Stand alone work-directed interventions A specific work-directed intervention alone may increase the number of sickness absence days compared with work-directed care as usual (SMD 0.39, 95% CI 0.04 to 0.74; 2 studies, low-certainty evidence) but probably does not lead to more people being off work within the first year of follow-up (RR 0.93, 95% CI 0.77 to 1.11; 1 study, moderate-certainty evidence) or beyond (RR 1.00, 95% CI 0.82 to 1.22; 2 studies, moderate-certainty evidence). There is probably no effect on depressive symptoms (SMD -0.10, 95% -0.30 CI to 0.10; 4 studies, moderate-certainty evidence) within the first year of follow-up and there may be no effect on depressive symptoms beyond that time (SMD 0.18, 95% CI -0.13 to 0.49; 1 study, low-certainty evidence). The intervention may also not lead to better work functioning (SMD -0.32, 95% CI -0.90 to 0.26; 1 study, low-certainty evidence) within the first year of follow-up.   Psychological interventions A psychological intervention, either face-to-face, or an E-mental health intervention, with or without professional guidance, may reduce the number of sickness absence days, compared with care as usual (SMD -0.15, 95% CI -0.28 to -0.03; 9 studies, low-certainty evidence). It may also reduce depressive symptoms (SMD -0.30, 95% CI -0.45 to -0.15, 8 studies, low-certainty evidence). We are uncertain whether these psychological interventions improve work ability (SMD -0.15 95% CI -0.46 to 0.57; 1 study; very low-certainty evidence). Psychological intervention combined with antidepressant medication Two studies compared the effect of a psychological intervention combined with antidepressants to antidepressants alone. One study combined psychodynamic therapy with tricyclic antidepressant (TCA) medication and another combined telephone-administered cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) with a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI). We are uncertain if this intervention reduces the number of sickness absence days (SMD -0.38, 95% CI -0.99 to 0.24; 2 studies, very low-certainty evidence) but found that there may be no effect on depressive symptoms (SMD -0.19, 95% CI -0.50 to 0.12; 2 studies, low-certainty evidence). Antidepressant medication only Three studies compared the effectiveness of SSRI to selective norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor (SNRI) medication on reducing sickness absence and yielded highly inconsistent results. Improved care Overall, interventions to improve care did not lead to fewer days of sickness absence, compared to care as usual (SMD -0.05, 95% CI -0.16 to 0.06; 7 studies, moderate-certainty evidence). However, in studies with a low risk of bias, the intervention probably leads to fewer days of sickness absence in the first year of follow-up (SMD -0.20, 95% CI -0.35 to -0.05; 2 studies; moderate-certainty evidence). Improved care probably leads to fewer depressive symptoms (SMD -0.21, 95% CI -0.35 to -0.07; 7 studies, moderate-certainty evidence) but may possibly lead to a decrease in work-functioning (SMD 0.5, 95% CI 0.34 to 0.66; 1 study; moderate-certainty evidence). Exercise Supervised strength exercise may reduce sickness absence, compared to relaxation (SMD -1.11; 95% CI -1.68 to -0.54; one study, low-certainty evidence). However, aerobic exercise probably is not more effective than relaxation or stretching (SMD -0.06; 95% CI -0.36 to 0.24; 2 studies, moderate-certainty evidence). Both studies found no differences between the two conditions in depressive symptoms.


AUTHORS' CONCLUSIONS
A combination of a work-directed intervention and a clinical intervention probably reduces the number of sickness absence days, but at the end of one year or longer follow-up, this does not lead to more people in the intervention group being at work. The intervention may also reduce depressive symptoms and probably increases work functioning more than care as usual. Specific work-directed interventions may not be more effective than usual work-directed care alone. Psychological interventions may reduce the number of sickness absence days, compared with care as usual. Interventions to improve clinical care probably lead to lower sickness absence and lower levels of depression, compared with care as usual. There was no evidence of a difference in effect on sickness absence of one antidepressant medication compared to another. Further research is needed to assess which combination of work-directed and clinical interventions works best.

Abstract:
<jats:p>The increase of chronic diseases worldwide impact quality of life, cause economic and medical costs, and make it necessary to look for strategies and solutions that allow people with chronic diseases (PwCDs) to lead an active working life. As part of the CHRODIS Plus Joint European Action project, a systematic review was conducted to identify studies of interventions that support the maintenance of work and return to work (RTW) among workers with chronic illnesses. These interventions should target employees with the following conditions: diabetes, cardiovascular diseases, metabolic vascular syndrome, respiratory diseases, musculoskeletal disorders, mental disorders, and neurological disorders. An extensive search was performed in PubMed, EMBASE, and PsycINFO for English language studies. Included in this review were 15 randomized controlled trials (RCT) for adult employees (aged 18+). We found that workplace-oriented and multidisciplinary programs are the most supportive to RTW and reducing the absence due to illness. In addition, cognitive behavioral therapies achieve positive results on RTW and sick leave. Finally, coaching is effective for the self-management of chronic disease and significantly improved perceptions of working capacity and fatigue.</jats:p>

Abstract:
BackgroundWork disability is a major personal, financial and public health burden. Predicting future work success is a major focus of research.ObjectivesTo identify common prognostic factors for return-to-work across different health and injury conditions and to describe their association with return-to-work outcomes.MethodsMedline, Embase, PsychINFO, Cinahl, and Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews and the grey literature were searched from January 1, 2004 to September 1, 2013. Systematic reviews addressing return-to-work in various conditions and injuries were selected. Eligible studies were critically appraised using the Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network criteria to identify low risk of bias reviews.ResultsOf the 36,193 titles screened and the 94 eligible studies reviewed, 56 systematic reviews were accepted as low risk of bias. Over half of these focused on musculoskeletal disorders, which were primarily spine related (e.g., neck and low back pain). The other half of studies assessed workers with mental health or cardiovascular conditions, stroke, cancer, multiple sclerosis or other non-specified health conditions. Many factors have been assessed, but few consistently across conditions. Common factors associated with positive return-to-work outcomes were higher education and socioeconomic status, higher self-efficacy and optimistic expectations for recovery and return-to-work, lower severity of the injury/illness, return-to-work coordination, and multidisciplinary interventions that include the workplace and stakeholders. Common factors associated with negative return-to-work outcomes were older age, being female, higher pain or disability, depression, higher physical work demands, previous sick leave and unemployment, and activity limitations.ConclusionsExpectations of recovery and return-to-work, pain and disability levels, depression, workplace factors, and access to multidisciplinary resources are important modifiable factors in progressing return-to-work across health and injury conditions. Employers, healthcare providers and other stakeholders can use this information to facilitate return-to-work for injured/ill workers regardless of the specific injury or illness. Future studies should investigate novel interventions, and other factors that may be common across health conditions.

Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000016

From text 'ryan2017webbasedinterventionsfor pages 3-5'.

Cathal Ryan, Michael Bergin, Trudie Chalder, and John S G Wells. Web-based interventions for the management of stress in the workplace: focus, form, and efficacy. Journal of Occupational Health, 59:215-236, May 2017. URL: https://doi.org/10.1539/joh.16-0227-ra, doi:10.1539/joh.16-0227-ra. This article has 68 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt summarizes randomized trials of CBT-based and web-based CBT/work-focused interventions in working samples. Formats included web-based programs (guided, self-guided/unguided), smartphone ACT-style apps, and a blended eHealth plus occupational physician intervention. Effect sizes for perceived stress and mental-health outcomes were generally in the moderate-to-large range (example pooled/reported effects d = 0.4–0.75; GET.ON Stress self-guided d = 0.96 post, d = 0.65 at 6 months; guided d = 0.79 post, d = 0.85 at 6 months). Work-focused CBT targeting sleep (GET.ON Recovery) showed very large reductions in insomnia (d ≈ 1.37–1.45) and related improvements, but no impact on absenteeism. Many trials reported improvements in mental-health, work-related skills, sleep and competences (d ranges reported), yet most found no improvements on absenteeism, presenteeism, work engagement, or physical HRQoL. One blended eHealth + physician trial found faster return-to-work and higher remission at 9 months versus controls, though long-term return-to-work and treatment response did not differ. A cluster RCT in nursing staff found both groups improved in work functioning with no significant between-group differences. Overall: consistent moderate–large effects on stress/symptoms, but limited and inconsistent evidence for objective work outcomes like sick leave or sustained return-to-work.

## pqac-00000017

From text 'asplund2023workfocusedversusgeneric pages 1-2'.

Robert Persson Asplund, Sofia Asplund, Helene von Buxhoeveden, Hanna Delby, Karin Eriksson, Maurits Svenning Gerhardsson, Joachim Palm, Thea Skyttberg, Julia Torstensson, Brjánn Ljótsson, Per Carlbring, and Gerhard Andersson. Work-focused versus generic internet-based interventions for employees with stress-related disorders: randomized controlled trial. Journal of Medical Internet Research, 25:e34446, Apr 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.2196/34446, doi:10.2196/34446. This article has 25 citations and is from a domain leading peer-reviewed journal.

This randomized trial (N=182 employees, mainly healthcare/IT/education) compared a 10-week internet-based work-focused CBT (W-iCBT) to a generic internet-based CBT (iCBT) and a waitlist control (WLC). Primary outcome was burnout measured with the Shirom-Melamed Burnout Questionnaire (SMBQ). Both W-iCBT and generic iCBT produced large and significant reductions in SMBQ versus WLC at posttreatment (Cohen d=1.00 and 0.83) and moderate effects maintained at 6-month follow-up (d=0.74 each). Moderate-to-large effects were also observed on secondary health- and work-related outcomes. Importantly, only the work-focused iCBT showed significant improvements in work ability and reductions in short-term sickness absence (445 fewer days vs WLC; 324 fewer days vs iCBT). There were no significant group differences for work experience or long-term sick leave. Thus, internet-delivered CBT (both generic and work-focused) reduced stress/burnout, while adding work-focused components appeared to confer additional benefits on short-term sickness absence and self-rated work ability.

## pqac-00000018

From text 'ebert2016selfguidedinternetbasedand pages 1-2'.

David Daniel Ebert, Elena Heber, Matthias Berking, Heleen Riper, Pim Cuijpers, Burkhardt Funk, and Dirk Lehr. Self-guided internet-based and mobile-based stress management for employees: results of a randomised controlled trial. Occupational and Environmental Medicine, 73:315-323, Feb 2016. URL: https://doi.org/10.1136/oemed-2015-103269, doi:10.1136/oemed-2015-103269. This article has 232 citations and is from a highest quality peer-reviewed journal.

This RCT evaluated a self-guided internet- and mobile-supported stress management intervention (iSMI) for employees (N=264; PSS-10 ≥22). The intervention comprised seven sessions plus one booster, focusing on problem-solving and emotion regulation. Participants were randomized to the iSMI or a 6-month wait-list control with treatment as usual. Primary outcome was perceived stress (PSS-10), assessed at baseline, 7 weeks (post-treatment) and 6 months. The iSMI produced large short-term reductions in perceived stress (7 weeks d=0.96, 95% CI 0.70–1.21) and sustained moderate reductions at 6 months (d=0.65, 95% CI 0.40–0.89) versus control. Secondary outcomes (depression, anxiety, emotional exhaustion, sleep problems, worry, mental health–related QoL, psychological detachment, emotion regulation skills) also improved with small-to-moderate effect sizes. Presenteeism improved, but at 6 months there were no significant differences for work engagement, physical health–related QoL, or absenteeism. Overall, the self-guided internet CBT-style program was effective for reducing perceived stress and several related mental and some work outcomes, but did not significantly lower absenteeism at 6 months.


---

## pqac-00000019

From text 'salomonsson2020effectsofcognitive pages 1-2'.

Sigrid Salomonsson, Fredrik Santoft, Elin Lindsäter, Kersti Ejeby, Martin Ingvar, Brjánn Ljótsson, Lars‐Göran Öst, Mats Lekander, and Erik Hedman‐Lagerlöf. Effects of cognitive behavioural therapy and return-to-work intervention for patients on sick leave due to stress-related disorders: results from a randomized trial. Scandinavian journal of psychology, 61:281-289, Nov 2020. URL: https://doi.org/10.1111/sjop.12590, doi:10.1111/sjop.12590. This article has 50 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

This randomized trial enrolled primary care patients on sick leave (N = 211) and randomized them to cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT; n = 64), a return-to-work intervention (RTW-I; n = 67), or a combined CBT+RTW-I condition (COMBO; n = 80). The paper reports subgroup analyses for patients with adjustment disorder or exhaustion disorder (the Stress subgroup, n = 152). Primary outcomes were blinded Clinician Severity Rating (CSR) of symptoms and sick leave registry data. For the Stress subgroup, CBT produced greater symptom reduction than RTW-I at posttreatment, while COMBO did not differ from either CBT or RTW-I. Despite symptom differences, there was no difference between the three treatments on sick leave in the Stress subgroup. An exploratory analysis in a different subgroup (patients with depression, anxiety or insomnia) suggested RTW-I shortened sick-leave duration faster than CBT. The authors conclude that CBT appears promising for reducing stress/exhaustion symptoms, but RTW-I may have advantages for return-to-work speed in other diagnostic subgroups; no clear sick-leave benefit of CBT over RTW-I was found in the Stress subgroup.

## pqac-00000020

From text 'lindsater2018internetbasedcognitivebehavioral pages 1-2'.

Elin Lindsäter, Erland Axelsson, Sigrid Salomonsson, Fredrik Santoft, Kersti Ejeby, Brjánn Ljótsson, Torbjörn Åkerstedt, Mats Lekander, and Erik Hedman-Lagerlöf. Internet-based cognitive behavioral therapy for chronic stress: a randomized controlled trial. Psychotherapy and Psychosomatics, 87:296-305, Jul 2018. URL: https://doi.org/10.1159/000490742, doi:10.1159/000490742. This article has 89 citations and is from a highest quality peer-reviewed journal.

This randomized controlled trial evaluated a 12-week therapist-guided internet-based CBT (ICBT) for chronic stress in a clinical sample of 100 adults diagnosed with adjustment disorder (AD) or exhaustion disorder (ED). Participants were randomized to ICBT (n=50) or a waitlist control (n=50). The primary outcome was perceived stress (PSS). Compared to the control, ICBT produced a large, significant reduction in perceived stress (d = 1.09) and moderate-to-large improvements across secondary symptom domains. These symptom improvements were maintained at 6-month follow-up. However, there was no significant between-group effect on functional impairment or work ability. The authors conclude that a relatively short ICBT is effective in reducing stress-related symptoms in this clinical sample and could increase treatment accessibility, but results need replication and further study to clarify relationships between symptom reduction and functional/work outcomes.


---

## pqac-00000021

From text 'ryan2017webbasedinterventionsfor pages 6-7'.

Cathal Ryan, Michael Bergin, Trudie Chalder, and John S G Wells. Web-based interventions for the management of stress in the workplace: focus, form, and efficacy. Journal of Occupational Health, 59:215-236, May 2017. URL: https://doi.org/10.1539/joh.16-0227-ra, doi:10.1539/joh.16-0227-ra. This article has 68 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt reports several randomized trials of CBT formats for working populations: blended workplace CBT (3‑hour group CBT plus three individualized e-mail CBT homework/feedback; n=261) produced significant reductions in depressive symptoms compared with controls and improved self-reported stress-control skills, but did not affect self‑esteem. A 120‑min group CBT plus 1 month of web‑based homework (n=215) produced a small significant increase in subjective work performance at 3 months versus controls (d = 0.31). An RCT of internet CBT (n=381) produced modest improvements in worker well‑being with small significant reductions in depressive symptoms postintervention (d = 0.14) and at 6 months (d = 0.16), plus small gains in dysfunctional attitudes, knowledge, and self‑efficacy; there were no significant effects on psychological distress or problem solving. A 3‑year follow-up of one intervention cohort showed larger uncontrolled gains (d = 1.3–1.8) on anxiety/stress and maintained improvements in depression and emotional exhaustion, but this follow-up was uncontrolled. Across these trials, work outcomes were limited: only subjective work performance was reported (small improvement); sick leave, return‑to‑work, or objective work‑ability outcomes are not reported in the excerpt.


---

## pqac-00000022

From text 'ryan2017webbasedinterventionsfor pages 5-6'.

Cathal Ryan, Michael Bergin, Trudie Chalder, and John S G Wells. Web-based interventions for the management of stress in the workplace: focus, form, and efficacy. Journal of Occupational Health, 59:215-236, May 2017. URL: https://doi.org/10.1539/joh.16-0227-ra, doi:10.1539/joh.16-0227-ra. This article has 68 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

Randomized trials of CBT for working populations in this excerpt tested primarily blended and purely online formats. One blended program (Mori et al.) combined a 2.5-hour group workplace CBT session with ~1 month of online CBT homework in 168 system engineers; it produced no overall effect on psychological distress versus controls but small improvements in thought transformation (d = 0.26) and coping at 6 months (d = 0.37). In a subgroup with clinically significant baseline distress (n=73) the blended intervention produced medium effects postintervention (d = 0.61) maintained at 6 months (d = 0.60). A purely e-mail CBT RCT (n=177) yielded significant medium-to-large improvements in stress, depression, anxiety, and emotional exhaustion versus control; odds of recovery from clinical stress were higher in the intervention group. Long-term follow-up (97 traced) showed large maintained gains (d = 1.3–1.8 at 3 years). Work-related outcomes (sick leave, return-to-work, work ability) are not reported in these CBT trials in the provided text; one other trial of online interventions found both arms improved in work functioning with no between-group differences.


---

## pqac-00000023

From text 'ruwaard2007e‐mailedstandardizedcognitive pages 1-4'.

Jeroen Ruwaard, Alfred Lange, Manon Bouwman, Janneke Broeksteeg, and Bart Schrieken. E‐mailed standardized cognitive behavioural treatment of work‐related stress: a randomized controlled trial. Cognitive Behaviour Therapy, 36:179-192, Nov 2007. URL: https://doi.org/10.1080/16506070701381863, doi:10.1080/16506070701381863. This article has 137 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

This randomized controlled trial tested a 7-week standardized cognitive behavioural treatment for work-related stress delivered via e-mail. From 342 applicants, 239 met inclusion criteria and were randomized to immediate treatment (n=177) or a waiting-list control (n=62). Primary psychological outcomes were measured with the DASS-42 and the Emotional Exhaustion scale of the MBI-GS. Attrition was 21% (50 participants). Intention-to-treat ANCOVAs showed that the treatment group improved significantly more than the waiting-list group. Within-group effects in the treatment arm were described as large-to-moderate (report gives "0.9 (stress)>d>0.5 (anxiety)"). Between-group effect sizes were reported as ranging from "d50.6 (stress) to d50.1 (anxiety)". A 3-year follow-up suggested more pronounced effects, but high attrition limits confidence. The paper recommends further research comparing Internet-driven CBT with face-to-face care and examining optimal therapist contact. The excerpt does not report work-specific outcomes (sick leave, return-to-work, or work ability) in the presented sections.

function.arguments: {"plan_objective_id": 2, "updated_plan_objective_json": "{\"id\":2,\"objective\":\"Identify high-quality peer-reviewed syntheses (systematic reviews/meta-analyses) on CBT for work-related stress and related occupational outcomes.\",\"rationale\":\"Syntheses provide the most reliable estimate of effectiveness and typical effect sizes across studies.\",\"status\":\"completed\",\"result\":\"Key syntheses identified and extracted: (1) Richardson & Rothstein 2008 meta-analysis of workplace stress-management interventions: overall moderate effect (d\u22480.53) with high heterogeneity; few studies measured absenteeism/productivity. (2) Klink et al. 2001 meta-analysis: CBT and multimodal interventions show moderate effects; organizational interventions largely non-significant. (3) Bhui et al. 2012 review of reviews: CBT programs tend to show larger individual-level mental-health effects than other approaches; evidence gaps for absenteeism. (4) Joyce et al. 2016 meta-review: consistent symptom reduction for CBT-based workplace interventions; occupational outcomes mixed and better when CBT is work-focused/RTW-oriented. (5) Ryan et al. 2017 systematic review of web-based workplace stress interventions: CBT/iCBT shows moderate-to-large effects on stress but inconsistent/limited effects on absenteeism. function.arguments: {"question": "In RCTs of workplace/work-related stress, what were the effects of CBT on sickness absence/return-to-work and symptom outcomes (stress, burnout/exhaustion, depression/anxiety)? Extract any quantitative results if available.", "text_name_focus_list": ["asplund2018internetbasedstressmanagement pages 1-2", "asplund2018internetbasedstressmanagement pages 6-7", "ebert2016selfguidedinternetbasedand pages 1-2", "ebert2016selfguidedinternetbasedand pages 7-8", "lindsater2018internetbasedcognitivebehavioral pages 1-2", "asplund2023workfocusedversusgeneric pages 1-2", "salomonsson2020effectsofcognitive pages 1-2", "ruwaard2007e\u2010mailedstandardizedcognitive pages 1-4"]}
function.name: gather_evidence

messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000024

From text 'salomonsson2020effectsofcognitive pages 1-2'.

Sigrid Salomonsson, Fredrik Santoft, Elin Lindsäter, Kersti Ejeby, Martin Ingvar, Brjánn Ljótsson, Lars‐Göran Öst, Mats Lekander, and Erik Hedman‐Lagerlöf. Effects of cognitive behavioural therapy and return-to-work intervention for patients on sick leave due to stress-related disorders: results from a randomized trial. Scandinavian journal of psychology, 61:281-289, Nov 2020. URL: https://doi.org/10.1111/sjop.12590, doi:10.1111/sjop.12590. This article has 50 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

This randomized trial (N = 211; CBT n=64, RTW-I n=67, COMBO n=80) analyzed a Stress subgroup (adjustment or exhaustion disorder; n = 152) with primary outcomes of Clinician Severity Rating (CSR) and registry sick-leave data. Subgroup analyses found that, for stress-related disorders, CBT produced greater symptom reduction than the return-to-work intervention (RTW-I) at posttreatment, while the combined intervention (COMBO) did not differ from either CBT or RTW-I. Despite symptom benefits, there were no differences between treatments on sick-leave outcomes within the Stress subgroup. An exploratory subgroup (patients with depression, anxiety or insomnia) suggested RTW-I reduced sick leave faster than CBT. The authors conclude CBT may be promising for stress and exhaustion disorder, but note prior meta-analyses have generally found no clear treatment effects for clinical stress/exhaustion and only small effects of psychological interventions on sick leave for common mental disorders. The excerpt does not provide numerical effect sizes or statistical estimates for symptom or sick-leave outcomes.


---

## pqac-00000025

From text 'xu2024theeffectivenessof pages 21-22'.

Huaying Xu, Jinxuan Cai, Rakshat Sawhney, Stephen Jiang, Nicholas Buys, and Jing Sun. The effectiveness of cognitive-behavioral therapy in helping people on sick leave to return to work: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Journal of Occupational Rehabilitation, pages 1-33, Apr 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/s10926-023-10116-4, doi:10.1007/s10926-023-10116-4. This article has 37 citations and is from a domain leading peer-reviewed journal.

In the included RCTs of CBT for work-related/sick-leave populations, CBT reduced sickness absence days and increased return-to-work. Across 15 studies (n=1727) CBT reduced sick-leave days by a mean of −3.649 (95% CI −5.253 to −2.046; p<0.001) with a moderate effect size (−0.395). Across 16 studies (n=2298) CBT increased number returning to work (mean difference 1.5, 95% CI 1.019–1.722; p<0.05). Heterogeneity was high for sick-leave days (I2=92.991) but low/non-significant for RTW (I2=32.998). Symptom outcomes categorized as “mental illness” (5 studies, n=1301) showed a small improvement (MD −0.810) and a small effect size (−0.196) per Table 2. Subgroup analyses found larger sick-leave reductions when interventions were face-to-face (MD −8.673), included stress-management components (MD −3.498), used homework (MD −2.615), mood-management (effect size −0.926), longer courses (≥16 weeks) or longer sessions (≥90 min), combined CBT (MD −14.785), or group delivery. The excerpt does not separately report burnout/exhaustion or detailed depression/anxiety-specific estimates beyond the “mental illness” category.


---

## pqac-00000026

From text 'asplund2023workfocusedversusgeneric pages 1-2'.

Robert Persson Asplund, Sofia Asplund, Helene von Buxhoeveden, Hanna Delby, Karin Eriksson, Maurits Svenning Gerhardsson, Joachim Palm, Thea Skyttberg, Julia Torstensson, Brjánn Ljótsson, Per Carlbring, and Gerhard Andersson. Work-focused versus generic internet-based interventions for employees with stress-related disorders: randomized controlled trial. Journal of Medical Internet Research, 25:e34446, Apr 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.2196/34446, doi:10.2196/34446. This article has 25 citations and is from a domain leading peer-reviewed journal.

This randomized trial compared a work-focused internet CBT (W-iCBT), a generic iCBT, and a waitlist control in employees with stress-related disorders. Both W-iCBT and generic iCBT produced large reductions in burnout (primary outcome, measured by SMBQ): posttreatment Cohen's d = 1.00 (W-iCBT) and 0.83 (iCBT), and at 6 months both d = 0.74. Secondary health- and work-related outcomes showed moderate-to-large effects overall. Importantly, only the work-focused intervention (W-iCBT) produced significant improvements in work ability and reduced short-term sickness absence: 445 fewer short-term sickness absence days versus waitlist and 324 fewer days versus generic iCBT. There were no significant differences in work experience or long-term sick leave. The excerpt does not provide separate quantitative results for depression or anxiety outcomes beyond the general statement about secondary health outcomes, so symptom-specific effect sizes for depression/anxiety are not available here.


---

## pqac-00000027

From text 'xu2024theeffectivenessof pages 27-28'.

Huaying Xu, Jinxuan Cai, Rakshat Sawhney, Stephen Jiang, Nicholas Buys, and Jing Sun. The effectiveness of cognitive-behavioral therapy in helping people on sick leave to return to work: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Journal of Occupational Rehabilitation, pages 1-33, Apr 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/s10926-023-10116-4, doi:10.1007/s10926-023-10116-4. This article has 37 citations and is from a domain leading peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt reports results from a meta-analysis of CBT-based interventions for employees on sick leave. Overall the authors state CBT "significantly increased the number of people who RTW and improved physical function," though no RCT-only RTW/sick-leave numeric estimate is provided in the excerpt. Depression outcomes (15 studies, n=2870) showed a mean reduction of −0.965 (95% CI −1.745 to −0.186; p<0.05) and a small standardized effect size of −0.176 (95% CI −0.322 to −0.031; p<0.05). Another reported mean reduction (unspecified outcome in excerpt) was −0.810 (95% CI −1.561 to −0.060; p<0.05) with SMD −0.196 (95% CI −0.308 to −0.083; p<0.01). Subgroup data for RCTs (13 RCTs, n≈2676) show small but significant post–pre depression effects (reported in table as MD ≈ −0.175 and SMD ≈ −0.154). Publication-bias testing (Egger) was non-significant for sick-leave reduced days (p=0.098) and overall minimal (p>0.05). The excerpt provides clear quantitative evidence of small improvements in depression/anxiety symptoms and a qualitative statement of increased RTW, but it lacks RCT-specific numeric estimates for RTW/sick-leave and for burnout/exhaustion or stress outcomes.


---

## pqac-00000028

From text 'xu2024theeffectivenessof pages 1-2'.

Huaying Xu, Jinxuan Cai, Rakshat Sawhney, Stephen Jiang, Nicholas Buys, and Jing Sun. The effectiveness of cognitive-behavioral therapy in helping people on sick leave to return to work: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Journal of Occupational Rehabilitation, pages 1-33, Apr 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/s10926-023-10116-4, doi:10.1007/s10926-023-10116-4. This article has 37 citations and is from a domain leading peer-reviewed journal.

This systematic review/meta-analysis pooled 34 RCTs of CBT-based interventions for employees on sick leave. Fifteen RCTs (n=1727) reported sickness absence and found a mean reduction in sick-leave days of −3.654 (95% CI −5.253 to −2.046; p<0.001). Sixteen papers (n=2298) reported return-to-work timing, with the intervention group returning about 1.5 days earlier (95% CI 1.019 to 1.722; p<0.05). Symptom outcomes: the authors report CBT-based interventions were effective for fatigue, mental illness, and depression and improved physical function, but showed no effects for stress, anxiety, or working ability. The review targeted studies of employees on sick leave for causes including depression, stress-related mental disorders, and chronic pain; the excerpt does not isolate results exclusively from RCTs of workplace/work-related stress, so direct applicability to workplace-specific stress trials is limited in this passage.


---

## pqac-00000029

From text 'joyce2016workplaceinterventionsfor pages 8-9'.

Sadhbh Joyce, Matthew Modini, H. Christensen, A. Mykletun, R. Bryant, P. Mitchell, and S. Harvey. Workplace interventions for common mental disorders: a systematic meta-review. Psychological Medicine, 46:683-697, Dec 2016. URL: https://doi.org/10.1017/s0033291715002408, doi:10.1017/s0033291715002408. This article has 712 citations and is from a highest quality peer-reviewed journal.

Across included reviews of workplace interventions, CBT shows consistent symptom reduction for common mental disorders (rated highly: "Symptom reduction: ⋆⋆⋆"). Reviews report significant improvements in anxiety and depression symptomology and promising effects as an early secondary intervention for reducing work-related stress. However, improvements in clinical symptoms did not reliably translate into work-related outcomes in general workplace stress trials (e.g. "this did not translate to notable improvements in work-related factors such as absenteeism and productivity"). Where occupational outcomes improve, it is typically when CBT is delivered as part of a return-to-work (RTW) programme or when therapy is explicitly work-focused (e.g. problem-focused CBT). One review reported a quantitative RTW benefit: CBT may facilitate an earlier RTW by an average of 2 weeks among employees with adjustment disorder. Overall, the evidence base is mixed in quality; some reviews concluded that study quality was low and prevented firm recommendations about organizational outcomes.


---

## pqac-00000030

From text 'ebert2016selfguidedinternetbasedand pages 1-2'.

David Daniel Ebert, Elena Heber, Matthias Berking, Heleen Riper, Pim Cuijpers, Burkhardt Funk, and Dirk Lehr. Self-guided internet-based and mobile-based stress management for employees: results of a randomised controlled trial. Occupational and Environmental Medicine, 73:315-323, Feb 2016. URL: https://doi.org/10.1136/oemed-2015-103269, doi:10.1136/oemed-2015-103269. This article has 232 citations and is from a highest quality peer-reviewed journal.

This randomized trial (N=264; PSS-10 ≥22) evaluated a self-guided internet/mobile stress-management intervention (iSMI: seven sessions + booster, including problem-solving and emotion-regulation techniques) versus a 6-month wait-list control. The primary outcome was perceived stress (PSS-10). The iSMI produced a large reduction in perceived stress at post-treatment (7 weeks) with d=0.96 (95% CI 0.70–1.21) and a moderate effect at 6 months, d=0.65 (95% CI 0.40–0.89). Secondary outcomes showed significant small-to-moderate improvements in depression, anxiety, emotional exhaustion (burnout-related), sleep problems, worrying, mental health–related quality of life, psychological detachment, emotion-regulation skills, and presenteeism in favor of the iSMI. However, at 6 months, absenteeism (sickness absence) did not differ significantly between groups, nor did work engagement or physical health–related quality of life. In sum, this trial shows meaningful symptom improvements (stress, burnout-related emotional exhaustion, depression/anxiety) but no detected effect on absenteeism at 6 months.


---

## pqac-00000031

From text 'xu2024theeffectivenessof pages 2-3'.

Huaying Xu, Jinxuan Cai, Rakshat Sawhney, Stephen Jiang, Nicholas Buys, and Jing Sun. The effectiveness of cognitive-behavioral therapy in helping people on sick leave to return to work: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Journal of Occupational Rehabilitation, pages 1-33, Apr 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/s10926-023-10116-4, doi:10.1007/s10926-023-10116-4. This article has 37 citations and is from a domain leading peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt summarizes a systematic review/meta-analysis protocol assessing RCTs of CBT for employees on sick leave, focusing on return-to-work (RTW) and days of sick leave as primary outcomes and symptoms (stress, anxiety, depression, fatigue, working ability, physical function) as secondary outcomes. Inclusion required RCTs of CBT with both cognitive and behavioral components in adult employees on workplace-related sick leave. The text reports mixed trial findings: some RCTs showed CBT significantly reduced duration of musculoskeletal (MSK)-related sick leaves and improved physical and mental health, and related interventions also showed benefits for RTW after mental-condition leaves (stress, burnout). However, other RCTs found no significant difference between CBT and care-as-usual despite improvements versus no-treatment controls. The excerpt does not provide quantitative effect sizes, confidence intervals, or pooled estimates — only qualitative statements about significance and inconsistency across studies. Searches covered multiple databases for studies from 1990 to mid-2022.

messages.content:
Updated plan:
{
  "objectives": [
    {
      "id": 1,
      "objective": "Define scope and key outcomes for CBT targeting workplace stress (e.g., perceived stress, burnout, anxiety/depression, absenteeism/work functioning) and inclusion criteria (peer-reviewed, working populations, CBT or CBT-based interventions).",
      "rationale": "Clarifies what counts as CBT for workplace stress and what ‘effectiveness’ means so evidence extraction is aligned with the user’s question.",
      "status": "completed",
      "result": "Scope defined as CBT or CBT-based stress management interventions delivered to working adults with work-related stress complaints or stress-related disorders (including internet-delivered CBT), with effectiveness judged on validated symptom outcomes (perceived stress, burnout/exhaustion, depression/anxiety) and occupational outcomes (sick leave/return-to-work, work ability, productivity/presenteeism).",
      "evaluation": "Establishes operational definitions and outcome domains used in subsequent evidence gathering."
    },
    {
      "id": 2,
      "objective": "Identify high-quality peer-reviewed syntheses (systematic reviews/meta-analyses) on CBT for work-related stress and related occupational outcomes.",
      "rationale": "Syntheses provide the most reliable estimate of effectiveness and typical effect sizes across studies.",
      "status": "completed",
      "result": "Key syntheses identified and extracted: (1) Richardson & Rothstein 2008 meta-analysis of workplace stress-management interventions: overall moderate effect (d≈0.53) with high heterogeneity; few studies measured absenteeism/productivity. (2) Klink et al. 2001 meta-analysis: CBT and multimodal interventions show moderate effects; organizational interventions largely non-significant. (3) Bhui et al. 2012 review of reviews: CBT programs tend to show larger individual-level mental-health effects than other approaches; evidence gaps for absenteeism. (4) Joyce et al. 2016 meta-review: consistent symptom reduction for CBT-based workplace interventions; occupational outcomes mixed and better when CBT is work-focused/RTW-oriented. (5) Ryan et al. 2017 systematic review of web-based workplace stress interventions: CBT/iCBT shows moderate-to-large effects on stress but inconsistent/limited effects on absenteeism. (6) Xu et al. 2024 meta-analysis of CBT for employees on sick leave: pooled reductions in sick-leave days (~3.7 days) and ~1.5 days earlier RTW; no pooled effect on stress/anxiety or working ability; small improvement in depression.",
      "evaluation": "Multiple peer-reviewed reviews/meta-analyses relevant to CBT for workplace stress and occupational outcomes were obtained, with quantitative estimates extracted where available."
    },
    {
      "id": 3,
      "objective": "Identify and extract evidence from key randomized controlled trials of CBT delivered in workplace/occupational contexts (face-to-face, group, online/iCBT), including outcomes and follow-up durability.",
      "rationale": "RCTs provide direct causal evidence and allow characterization of intervention formats and magnitude/duration of benefit.",
      "status": "completed",
      "result": "Extracted RCT evidence emphasizing occupational stress samples and stress-related disorders: (1) Ebert et al. 2016 self-guided iSMI (CBT-based) in employees with elevated stress: large stress reduction post (d=0.96) and moderate at 6 months (d=0.65), plus improvements in emotional exhaustion and depression/anxiety; no absenteeism effect at 6 months. (2) Lindsäter et al. 2018 therapist-guided iCBT for adjustment/exhaustion disorder: large perceived-stress reduction vs waitlist (d=1.09) maintained at 6 months; no work ability/functional impairment effect. (3) Ruwaard et al. 2007 e-mail CBT for work-related stress: significant improvements vs waitlist, with within-group effects ~d 0.5–0.9 and between-group effects ~d 0.1–0.6; no work outcomes reported. (4) Asplund et al. 2023 employees with stress-related disorders: work-focused iCBT and generic iCBT both reduced burnout strongly (post d=1.00 and 0.83; 6-month d=0.74), and only work-focused iCBT improved work ability and reduced short-term sickness absence (hundreds of days fewer). (5) Salomonsson et al. 2020 sick-listed stress subgroup: CBT reduced symptoms more than RTW intervention posttreatment but did not reduce sick leave relative to RTW intervention.",
      "evaluation": "Includes multiple peer-reviewed RCTs in working populations and stress-related disorders with quantitative effect sizes and follow-up where available, covering both symptom and work outcomes."
    },
    {
      "id": 4,
      "objective": "Retrieve at least one relevant figure/table via image extraction to support quantitative results (effect sizes, forest plot, or key outcome table).",

      "result": null,

    },
    {

      "rationale": "The user asks what the literature says; synthesis is needed beyond listing studies.",

      "result": null,
      "evaluation": null
    },
    {
      "id": 6,
      "objective": "Create at least one artifact (e.g., summary table of key reviews/RCTs and effects) for inclusion in final answer.",
      "rationale": "Artifacts improve clarity and ensure the final response is a technical document with organized evidence.",
      "status": "pending",
      "result": null,
      "evaluation": null
    },
    {
      "id": 7,
      "objective": "Draft and submit the final cited answer using the answer tool.",

## Context ID: pqac-00000032

Both the table and the forest plots show that CBT-based interventions significantly reduced sick leave days (mean reduction -3.649) and increased the likelihood of return-to-work (odds ratio 1.5).

function.arguments: {"artifact_type": "table", "description": "Create a compact markdown table summarizing key peer-reviewed evidence on CBT for workplace/work-related stress. Include rows for: Richardson & Rothstein 2008 (meta-analysis), van der Klink/Klink et al. 2001 (meta-analysis), Bhui et al. 2012 (review of reviews), Joyce et al. 2016 (meta-review), Ryan et al. 2017 (systematic review web-based), Xu et al. 2024 (RTW meta-analysis), and key RCTs: Ebert 2016, Linds\u00e4ter 2018, Ruwaard 2007, Asplund 2023, Salomonsson 2020. Columns: Study type/population, CBT format, Main outcomes, Quantitative effect (effect sizes/MD), Durability/follow-up, Notes on work outcomes."}
function.name: create_artifact

messages.content:
### Artifact (ID artifact-00)

| Study | Study type / population | CBT format | Main outcomes | Quantitative effect | Durability / follow-up | Notes on work outcomes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Richardson & Rothstein 2008 | Meta-analysis of occupational stress-management interventions in workers | CBT, multimodal, relaxation, organizational programs | Overall stress/mental-health outcomes | Overall pooled effect d≈0.53; high heterogeneity (I²≈73%); few absenteeism/productivity studies (pqac-00000015) | Not summarized as a single follow-up estimate in excerpt | Supports moderate overall benefit of stress-management interventions, but evidence on absenteeism/productivity is sparse (pqac-00000015) |
| van der Klink / Klink et al. 2001 | Meta-analysis of 48 experimental studies of work-related stress interventions | Cognitive-behavioural, multimodal, relaxation, organizational | Complaints, psychological resources/responses, quality of work life | CBT and multimodal interventions showed moderate effects; organizational interventions non-significant; excerpt does not provide exact pooled d by CBT subgroup (pqac-00000013) | Not specified in excerpt | Stronger evidence for individual than organizational outcomes; work-outcome estimates not detailed (pqac-00000013) |
| Bhui et al. 2012 | Review of 23 systematic reviews / 499 primary studies | Individual interventions including CBT | Anxiety, depression, absenteeism | CBT programs repeatedly showed larger individual-level mental-health effects than other approaches; no numeric pooled CBT estimate in excerpt (pqac-00000014) | Review-level synthesis; no single durability estimate | Evidence for absenteeism is limited; organizational interventions mixed (pqac-00000014) |
| Joyce et al. 2016 | Systematic meta-review of workplace interventions for common mental disorders | CBT-based workplace and RTW-focused interventions | Symptom reduction, absenteeism, productivity, RTW | Consistent symptom reduction; one cited Cochrane review suggested earlier RTW by ~2 weeks; occupational effects otherwise mixed (pqac-00000009, pqac-00000029) | Depends on underlying reviews | Work-focused / RTW-oriented CBT appears more likely to improve occupational outcomes than generic CBT (pqac-00000009, pqac-00000029) |
| Ryan et al. 2017 | Systematic review of web-based workplace stress interventions | Web-based CBT / iCBT / blended CBT | Stress, mental health, insomnia, work functioning | Across trials, effects ranged from about d=0.3 to >1.0; examples include perceived stress d=0.96 post and d=0.65 at 6 months in GET.ON Stress-related trials (pqac-00000011, pqac-00000016) | Some studies reported 6-month maintenance; uncontrolled 3-year follow-up in one trial (pqac-00000011, pqac-00000022) | Work effects inconsistent; many studies found no absenteeism/presenteeism benefit, though some showed faster RTW or small work-performance gains (pqac-00000011, pqac-00000016, pqac-00000021) |
| Xu et al. 2024 | Systematic review and meta-analysis of 34 RCTs in employees on sick leave | CBT-based interventions, including stress-management and RTW-oriented variants | Sick leave, RTW, depression, stress/anxiety, work ability | Sick leave reduced by MD −3.654 days (95% CI −5.253 to −2.046); RTW ~1.5 days earlier; depression small benefit (effect sizes ~−0.176 to −0.196); no pooled effect for stress, anxiety, or working ability (pqac-00000008, pqac-00000010, pqac-00000025, pqac-00000028, pqac-00000032) | Pooled across varied follow-up intervals; figure/table extracted from review (pqac-00000032, pqac-00000033) | Best quantitative evidence for occupational outcomes, but heterogeneity is high for sick-leave reduction (pqac-00000025, pqac-00000032) |
| Ebert 2016 | RCT in employees with elevated perceived stress (N=264) | Self-guided internet/mobile CBT-based stress management | Perceived stress, depression/anxiety, emotional exhaustion, presenteeism, absenteeism | Perceived stress d=0.96 post, d=0.65 at 6 months; secondary outcomes improved with small-to-moderate effects (pqac-00000018, pqac-00000030) | 6-month follow-up with maintained moderate stress benefit (pqac-00000018, pqac-00000030) | Improved presenteeism, but no significant absenteeism effect at 6 months (pqac-00000018, pqac-00000030) |
| Lindsäter 2018 | RCT in clinical stress-related disorders / adjustment or exhaustion disorder (N=100) | Therapist-guided internet CBT | Perceived stress, secondary stress-related symptoms, work ability | Large stress reduction vs waitlist, d=1.09; moderate-to-large secondary symptom improvements (pqac-00000005, pqac-00000020) | Effects maintained at 6 months (pqac-00000005, pqac-00000020) | No significant effect on work ability or functional impairment (pqac-00000005, pqac-00000020) |
| Ruwaard 2007 | RCT in adults with work-related stress (n=239 randomized) | 7-week e-mailed standardized CBT | Stress, depression, anxiety, emotional exhaustion | Within-group effects ~d=0.5–0.9; between-group effects ~d=0.1–0.6, largest for stress (pqac-00000004, pqac-00000023) | Follow-up suggested more pronounced effects, but attrition limited interpretation (pqac-00000004, pqac-00000023) | No work-specific outcomes reported in extracted sections (pqac-00000004, pqac-00000023) |
| Asplund 2023 | RCT in employees with stress-related disorders (N=182) | Work-focused iCBT vs generic iCBT vs waitlist | Burnout, work ability, sickness absence | Burnout post: d=1.00 (work-focused) and 0.83 (generic); both d=0.74 at 6 months; only work-focused iCBT improved work ability and reduced short-term sickness absence (445 fewer days vs waitlist; 324 fewer vs generic iCBT) (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000017, pqac-00000026) | 6-month benefits maintained; 12-month assessment mentioned in trial (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000017) | Strong evidence that adding work-focused components may improve short-term occupational outcomes beyond symptom relief (pqac-00000017, pqac-00000026) |
| Salomonsson 2020 | Randomized trial in primary-care patients on sick leave; stress subgroup with adjustment/exhaustion disorder (n=152 within larger N=211 trial) | Face-to-face CBT vs RTW intervention vs combined | Symptom severity, registry sick leave | CBT reduced symptoms more than RTW intervention posttreatment; no numeric effect size in excerpt; no sick-leave difference between groups (pqac-00000019, pqac-00000024) | Posttreatment and registry follow-up reported in trial, but no compact numeric durability estimate in excerpt | Illustrates common pattern: symptom benefit without clear added sick-leave benefit for CBT alone (pqac-00000019, pqac-00000024) |


*Table: This table summarizes major peer-reviewed reviews and randomized trials on CBT for workplace or work-related stress. It highlights that CBT generally improves stress and related symptoms, while work outcomes such as absenteeism and return-to-work improve less consistently unless interventions are explicitly work-focused.*

Artifact IDs that may be injected into the answer: [artifact-00]

Context IDs that may be cited: [pqac-00000000, pqac-00000001, pqac-00000002, pqac-00000003, pqac-00000004, pqac-00000005, pqac-00000006, pqac-00000007, pqac-00000008, pqac-00000009, pqac-00000010, pqac-00000011, pqac-00000012, pqac-00000013, pqac-00000014, pqac-00000015, pqac-00000016, pqac-00000017, pqac-00000018, pqac-00000019, pqac-00000020, pqac-00000021, pqac-00000022, pqac-00000023, pqac-00000024, pqac-00000025, pqac-00000026, pqac-00000027, pqac-00000028, pqac-00000029, pqac-00000030, pqac-00000031, pqac-00000032, pqac-00000033]

Status: Paper Count=69 | Relevant Papers=11 | Clinical Trial Count=0 | Relevant Clinical Trials=0 | Current Evidence=34 | Current Cost=$0.2095 | Disease-Target Evidence=0

function.arguments: {"plan_objective_id": 5, "updated_plan_objective_json": "{\"id\":5,\"objective\":\"Synthesize findings into a structured conclusion about effectiveness, moderators (delivery mode, population, baseline severity), and limitations (risk of bias, heterogeneity, generalizability).\",\"rationale\":\"The user asks what the literature says; synthesis is needed beyond listing studies.\",\"status\":\"completed\",\"result\":\"Synthesis: (a) Symptom outcomes\u2014Across meta-analyses and RCTs, CBT-based interventions for work-related stress generally yield small-to-moderate average improvements, with some trials showing large effects on perceived stress/burnout (notably internet-based stress management programs). (b) Durability\u2014Benefits on stress/burnout commonly persist at least 6 months in iCBT/iSMI trials. (c) Work outcomes\u2014Evidence for absenteeism/RTW/work ability is weaker and inconsistent; pooled evidence in sick-listed samples shows small improvements in sick-leave days/RTW, but in occupational stress trials CBT often improves symptoms without changing absenteeism/work ability unless work-focused/RTW elements are included. (d) Moderators\u2014Greater work-outcome effects appear when CBT is explicitly work-focused (integrates work ability, workplace contact, problem-solving for work issues), and when baseline distress is clinically significant; guided formats can have similar or somewhat larger effects than unguided. (e) Limitations\u2014High heterogeneity, varied comparators and outcome measures, limited objective work-outcome reporting, and attrition in digital trials constrain certainty and generalizability.\",\"evaluation\":\"Objective met: integrates review-level and RCT evidence into coherent conclusions, including moderators and limitations aligned to workplace stress effectiveness question.\"}"}

messages.content:
Updated plan:
{
  "objectives": [
    {
      "id": 1,
      "objective": "Define scope and key outcomes for CBT targeting workplace stress (e.g., perceived stress, burnout, anxiety/depression, absenteeism/work functioning) and inclusion criteria (peer-reviewed, working populations, CBT or CBT-based interventions).",
      "rationale": "Clarifies what counts as CBT for workplace stress and what ‘effectiveness’ means so evidence extraction is aligned with the user’s question.",
      "status": "completed",
      "result": "Scope defined as CBT or CBT-based stress management interventions delivered to working adults with work-related stress complaints or stress-related disorders (including internet-delivered CBT), with effectiveness judged on validated symptom outcomes (perceived stress, burnout/exhaustion, depression/anxiety) and occupational outcomes (sick leave/return-to-work, work ability, productivity/presenteeism).",
      "evaluation": "Establishes operational definitions and outcome domains used in subsequent evidence gathering."
    },
    {
      "id": 2,
      "objective": "Identify high-quality peer-reviewed syntheses (systematic reviews/meta-analyses) on CBT for work-related stress and related occupational outcomes.",
      "rationale": "Syntheses provide the most reliable estimate of effectiveness and typical effect sizes across studies.",
      "status": "completed",
      "result": "Key syntheses identified and extracted: (1) Richardson & Rothstein 2008 meta-analysis of workplace stress-management interventions: overall moderate effect (d≈0.53) with high heterogeneity; few studies measured absenteeism/productivity. (2) Klink et al. 2001 meta-analysis: CBT and multimodal interventions show moderate effects; organizational interventions largely non-significant. (3) Bhui et al. 2012 review of reviews: CBT programs tend to show larger individual-level mental-health effects than other approaches; evidence gaps for absenteeism. (4) Joyce et al. 2016 meta-review: consistent symptom reduction for CBT-based workplace interventions; occupational outcomes mixed and better when CBT is work-focused/RTW-oriented. (5) Ryan et al. 2017 systematic review of web-based workplace stress interventions: CBT/iCBT shows moderate-to-large effects on stress but inconsistent/limited effects on absenteeism. (6) Xu et al. 2024 meta-analysis of CBT for employees on sick leave: pooled reductions in sick-leave days (~3.7 days) and ~1.5 days earlier RTW; no pooled effect on stress/anxiety or working ability; small improvement in depression.",
      "evaluation": "Multiple peer-reviewed reviews/meta-analyses relevant to CBT for workplace stress and occupational outcomes were obtained, with quantitative estimates extracted where available."
    },
    {
      "id": 3,
      "objective": "Identify and extract evidence from key randomized controlled trials of CBT delivered in workplace/occupational contexts (face-to-face, group, online/iCBT), including outcomes and follow-up durability.",
      "rationale": "RCTs provide direct causal evidence and allow characterization of intervention formats and magnitude/duration of benefit.",
      "status": "completed",
      "result": "Extracted RCT evidence emphasizing occupational stress samples and stress-related disorders: (1) Ebert et al. 2016 self-guided iSMI (CBT-based) in employees with elevated stress: large stress reduction post (d=0.96) and moderate at 6 months (d=0.65), plus improvements in emotional exhaustion and depression/anxiety; no absenteeism effect at 6 months. (2) Lindsäter et al. 2018 therapist-guided iCBT for adjustment/exhaustion disorder: large perceived-stress reduction vs waitlist (d=1.09) maintained at 6 months; no work ability/functional impairment effect. (3) Ruwaard et al. 2007 e-mail CBT for work-related stress: significant improvements vs waitlist, with within-group effects ~d 0.5–0.9 and between-group effects ~d 0.1–0.6; no work outcomes reported. (4) Asplund et al. 2023 employees with stress-related disorders: work-focused iCBT and generic iCBT both reduced burnout strongly (post d=1.00 and 0.83; 6-month d=0.74), and only work-focused iCBT improved work ability and reduced short-term sickness absence (hundreds of days fewer). (5) Salomonsson et al. 2020 sick-listed stress subgroup: CBT reduced symptoms more than RTW intervention posttreatment but did not reduce sick leave relative to RTW intervention.",
      "evaluation": "Includes multiple peer-reviewed RCTs in working populations and stress-related disorders with quantitative effect sizes and follow-up where available, covering both symptom and work outcomes."
    },
    {
      "id": 4,
      "objective": "Retrieve at least one relevant figure/table via image extraction to support quantitative results (effect sizes, forest plot, or key outcome table).",
      "rationale": "The tool guidance requests using view_images at least once; figures/tables often summarize effect sizes and enhance evidentiary support.",
      "status": "completed",
      "result": "Retrieved a pooled-effect forest plot and summary table (Figure 2a and Table 2) from Xu et al. 2024 meta-analysis showing effects of CBT-based interventions on reduced sick-leave days and return-to-work.",
      "evaluation": "Objective met: at least one relevant figure/table was extracted and can be cited in final answer."
    },
    {
      "id": 5,
      "objective": "Synthesize findings into a structured conclusion about effectiveness, moderators (delivery mode, population, baseline severity), and limitations (risk of bias, heterogeneity, generalizability).",
      "rationale": "The user asks what the literature says; synthesis is needed beyond listing studies.",
      "status": "completed",
      "result": "Synthesis: (a) Symptom outcomes—Across meta-analyses and RCTs, CBT-based interventions for work-related stress generally yield small-to-moderate average improvements, with some trials showing large effects on perceived stress/burnout (notably internet-based stress management programs). (b) Durability—Benefits on stress/burnout commonly persist at least 6 months in iCBT/iSMI trials. (c) Work outcomes—Evidence for absenteeism/RTW/work ability is weaker and inconsistent; pooled evidence in sick-listed samples shows small improvements in sick-leave days/RTW, but in occupational stress trials CBT often improves symptoms without changing absenteeism/work ability unless work-focused/RTW elements are included. (d) Moderators—Greater work-outcome effects appear when CBT is explicitly work-focused (integrates work ability, workplace contact, problem-solving for work issues), and when baseline distress is clinically significant; guided formats can have similar or somewhat larger effects than unguided. (e) Limitations—High heterogeneity, varied comparators and outcome measures, limited objective work-outcome reporting, and attrition in digital trials constrain certainty and generalizability.",
      "evaluation": "Objective met: integrates review-level and RCT evidence into coherent conclusions, including moderators and limitations aligned to workplace stress effectiveness question."
    },
    {

      "objective": "Create at least one artifact (e.g., summary table of key reviews/RCTs and effects) for inclusion in final answer.",

(2) Lindsäter et al. 2018 therapist-guided iCBT for adjustment/exhaustion disorder: large perceived-stress reduction vs waitlist (d=1.09) maintained at 6 months; no work ability/functional impairment effect. (3) Ruwaard et al. 2007 e-mail CBT for work-related stress: significant improvements vs waitlist, with within-group effects ~d 0.5–0.9 and between-group effects ~d 0.1–0.6; no work outcomes reported. (4) Asplund et al. 2023 employees with stress-related disorders: work-focused iCBT and generic iCBT both reduced burnout strongly (post d=1.00 and 0.83; 6-month d=0.74), and only work-focused iCBT improved work ability and reduced short-term sickness absence (hundreds of days fewer). (5) Salomonsson et al. 2020 sick-listed stress subgroup: CBT reduced symptoms more than RTW intervention posttreatment but did not reduce sick leave relative to RTW intervention.",

function.arguments:
{"answer": "## Technical summary

Peer-reviewed evidence indicates that cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT)–based interventions can reduce *work-related stress symptoms* (perceived stress, burnout/exhaustion and related distress) in employees, with effects often in the small-to-moderate range on average but sometimes large in specific internet-delivered programs and higher-symptom samples. Evidence that CBT reliably improves *work outcomes* (absenteeism/sick leave, return-to-work [RTW], work ability) is weaker and more variable, with better occupational effects when CBT is explicitly work-focused or embedded in RTW-oriented programs. (pqac-00000009, pqac-00000014, pqac-00000015)

<artifact id="artifact-00"/>

## 1) Effectiveness for stress and burnout/exhaustion symptoms

### Evidence from workplace stress meta-analyses and meta-reviews

Meta-analytic and “review of reviews” evidence consistently places CBT-style programs among the more effective *individual-level* approaches for improving mental health outcomes in the context of work stress. A large meta-analysis of occupational stress management interventions reported an overall moderate pooled effect (d≈0.53), but with substantial heterogeneity and limited data on objective work outcomes. (pqac-00000015)

An earlier meta-analysis similarly concluded that cognitive–behavioural and multimodal programs show stronger effects than relaxation-only and organizational-level interventions (organizational interventions often non-significant), supporting CBT as one of the more effective individual-level strategies for work-related stress reduction. (pqac-00000013)

A meta-review of workplace interventions for common mental disorders (CMDs) concluded that CBT-based interventions produce robust symptom reduction, including in work-related stress presentations, but also emphasized that occupational outcomes are less consistently improved. (pqac-00000009, pqac-00000012)

### Evidence from trials of internet-delivered CBT/stress-management programs in employees

Multiple RCTs of CBT-based internet stress management show moderate-to-large between-group effects on perceived stress. For example, a self-guided internet/mobile stress-management intervention in employees with elevated stress produced a large reduction in perceived stress at post-treatment (d=0.96) and a sustained moderate effect at 6 months (d=0.65), with additional improvements in emotional exhaustion and depression/anxiety outcomes. (pqac-00000018, pqac-00000030)

In a clinical sample with stress-related disorders (adjustment disorder or exhaustion disorder), therapist-guided iCBT produced a large reduction in perceived stress versus waitlist (d=1.09), maintained at 6 months, indicating clinically meaningful symptom improvement in more severe, diagnosed stress conditions. (pqac-00000005, pqac-00000020)

In employed samples with stress-related disorders, internet-based CBT can also produce large reductions in burnout/exhaustion. In a three-arm RCT, both work-focused and generic iCBT reduced burnout strongly versus waitlist (post-treatment d=1.00 and d=0.83), with effects maintained at 6 months (both d=0.74). (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000026)

## 2) Effects on work outcomes (absenteeism/sick leave, RTW, work ability)

### Work outcomes in occupational stress trials: mixed and often null

Across workplace CBT/interventions reviews, symptom reductions often do *not* translate into consistent improvements in absenteeism or productivity; occupational benefits are more likely when CBT is work-focused or delivered within RTW programs. (pqac-00000009, pqac-00000029)

Consistent with this, in the employee iSMI RCT, presenteeism improved but absenteeism did not differ at 6 months, despite robust stress symptom reductions. (pqac-00000018, pqac-00000030)

In the clinical stress-disorder iCBT trial, symptom reductions did not correspond to measurable improvements in work ability or functional impairment. (pqac-00000005, pqac-00000020)

In sick-listed patients with adjustment/exhaustion disorder (stress subgroup), CBT reduced symptoms more than a RTW intervention at post-treatment, but there was no between-group difference in registry-based sick leave. (pqac-00000024)

### Work-focused CBT may yield better occupational effects

Work-focused elements appear to be a key moderator for occupational outcomes. In the RCT comparing work-focused iCBT vs generic iCBT vs waitlist, only the work-focused iCBT group showed significant effects on work ability and short-term sickness absence (hundreds of days fewer short-term sickness absence days than controls), despite both CBT arms showing similar burnout symptom improvements. (pqac-00000026)

### Pooled evidence in sick-listed workers: small but significant RTW/absence benefits

A 2024 systematic review and meta-analysis of 34 RCTs of CBT-based interventions in employees on sick leave found statistically significant but modest pooled improvements on occupational endpoints: 

- **Reduced sick leave days**: mean reduction ≈ −3.65 days (95% CI −5.25 to −2.05), with high heterogeneity. (pqac-00000028, pqac-00000025)
- **Earlier RTW**: ~1.5 days earlier RTW (as reported in the review’s results and forest plot/table). (pqac-00000028, pqac-00000032)

The review also found small improvements in depression outcomes but reported no pooled effects for stress, anxiety, or working ability in that sick-leave–focused evidence base. (pqac-00000028, pqac-00000027)

Figure/table evidence supporting these pooled work-outcome findings is visible in the extracted forest plot and summary table. (pqac-00000032, pqac-00000033)

## 3) Key moderators and implementation-relevant patterns

1. **Work-focus matters for work outcomes**: Reviews and RCTs converge on the pattern that CBT reliably improves symptoms, while effects on work outcomes are stronger when interventions include explicit work-directed components (work ability, workplace contact, RTW planning, work-problem solving). (pqac-00000009, pqac-00000026)
2. **Baseline severity/clinical distress may moderate effects**: Web-based workplace CBT trials show larger effects in clinically distressed subgroups than in unselected worker samples. (pqac-00000022)
3. **Durability**: Several internet-delivered CBT/stress-management trials report maintenance of symptom benefits through at least 6 months. (pqac-00000018, pqac-00000020)

## 4) Limitations of the evidence base

- **High heterogeneity** across workplace stress intervention studies and across CBT intervention variants limits generalizability of any single pooled effect estimate. (pqac-00000015, pqac-00000025)
- **Work outcomes under-measured**: classic occupational stress meta-analyses note relatively few studies measuring absenteeism/productivity, and many web-based stress trials report no objective work endpoints. (pqac-00000015, pqac-00000011)
- **Comparator effects and natural recovery**: in sick-listed stress populations, symptom improvement may occur in multiple arms over time, reducing detectable between-group differences in sick leave. (pqac-00000024)

## Conclusion

Peer-reviewed literature supports CBT (including internet-delivered CBT and CBT-based stress management) as an effective approach for reducing workplace stress symptoms and burnout-related outcomes, with moderate average effects and several trials reporting large symptom improvements sustained for months. (pqac-00000013, pqac-00000015, pqac-00000018, pqac-00000020)

However, translating symptom improvement into reduced absenteeism and improved work functioning is less consistent. The strongest evidence for occupational endpoints comes from sick-leave/RTW-focused CBT trials pooled in meta-analysis (showing small but significant reductions in sick-leave days and slightly earlier RTW), and from trials where CBT is explicitly *work-focused*, which appear more likely to improve work ability and short-term sickness absence. (pqac-00000025, pqac-00000026, pqac-00000032)"}

state.question: What does peer-reviewed literature say about the effectiveness of cognitive behavioural therapy for workplace stress?

answer.question: What does peer-reviewed literature say about the effectiveness of cognitive behavioural therapy for workplace stress?
answer.answer:
## Technical summary

Peer-reviewed evidence indicates that cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT)–based interventions can reduce *work-related stress symptoms* (perceived stress, burnout/exhaustion and related distress) in employees, with effects often in the small-to-moderate range on average but sometimes large in specific internet-delivered programs and higher-symptom samples. Evidence that CBT reliably improves *work outcomes* (absenteeism/sick leave, return-to-work [RTW], work ability) is weaker and more variable, with better occupational effects when CBT is explicitly work-focused or embedded in RTW-oriented programs. (joyce2016workplaceinterventionsfor pages 8-9, bhui2012asynthesisof pages 1-2, richardson2008effectsofoccupational pages 14-15)

| Study | Study type / population | CBT format | Main outcomes | Quantitative effect | Durability / follow-up | Notes on work outcomes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Richardson & Rothstein 2008 | Meta-analysis of occupational stress-management interventions in workers | CBT, multimodal, relaxation, organizational programs | Overall stress/mental-health outcomes | Overall pooled effect d≈0.53; high heterogeneity (I²≈73%); few absenteeism/productivity studies (richardson2008effectsofoccupational pages 14-15) | Not summarized as a single follow-up estimate in excerpt | Supports moderate overall benefit of stress-management interventions, but evidence on absenteeism/productivity is sparse (richardson2008effectsofoccupational pages 14-15) |
| van der Klink / Klink et al. 2001 | Meta-analysis of 48 experimental studies of work-related stress interventions | Cognitive-behavioural, multimodal, relaxation, organizational | Complaints, psychological resources/responses, quality of work life | CBT and multimodal interventions showed moderate effects; organizational interventions non-significant; excerpt does not provide exact pooled d by CBT subgroup (klink2001thebenefitsof pages 1-2) | Not specified in excerpt | Stronger evidence for individual than organizational outcomes; work-outcome estimates not detailed (klink2001thebenefitsof pages 1-2) |
| Bhui et al. 2012 | Review of 23 systematic reviews / 499 primary studies | Individual interventions including CBT | Anxiety, depression, absenteeism | CBT programs repeatedly showed larger individual-level mental-health effects than other approaches; no numeric pooled CBT estimate in excerpt (bhui2012asynthesisof pages 1-2) | Review-level synthesis; no single durability estimate | Evidence for absenteeism is limited; organizational interventions mixed (bhui2012asynthesisof pages 1-2) |
| Joyce et al. 2016 | Systematic meta-review of workplace interventions for common mental disorders | CBT-based workplace and RTW-focused interventions | Symptom reduction, absenteeism, productivity, RTW | Consistent symptom reduction; one cited Cochrane review suggested earlier RTW by ~2 weeks; occupational effects otherwise mixed (joyce2016workplaceinterventionsfor pages 8-9) | Depends on underlying reviews | Work-focused / RTW-oriented CBT appears more likely to improve occupational outcomes than generic CBT (joyce2016workplaceinterventionsfor pages 8-9) |
| Ryan et al. 2017 | Systematic review of web-based workplace stress interventions | Web-based CBT / iCBT / blended CBT | Stress, mental health, insomnia, work functioning | Across trials, effects ranged from about d=0.3 to >1.0; examples include perceived stress d=0.96 post and d=0.65 at 6 months in GET.ON Stress-related trials (ryan2017webbasedinterventionsfor pages 3-5) | Some studies reported 6-month maintenance; uncontrolled 3-year follow-up in one trial (ryan2017webbasedinterventionsfor pages 3-5, ryan2017webbasedinterventionsfor pages 5-6) | Work effects inconsistent; many studies found no absenteeism/presenteeism benefit, though some showed faster RTW or small work-performance gains (ryan2017webbasedinterventionsfor pages 3-5, ryan2017webbasedinterventionsfor pages 6-7) |
| Xu et al. 2024 | Systematic review and meta-analysis of 34 RCTs in employees on sick leave | CBT-based interventions, including stress-management and RTW-oriented variants | Sick leave, RTW, depression, stress/anxiety, work ability | Sick leave reduced by MD −3.654 days (95% CI −5.253 to −2.046); RTW ~1.5 days earlier; depression small benefit (effect sizes ~−0.176 to −0.196); no pooled effect for stress, anxiety, or working ability (xu2024theeffectivenessof pages 1-2, xu2024theeffectivenessof pages 27-28, xu2024theeffectivenessof pages 21-22, xu2024theeffectivenessof media f6fdbb57) | Pooled across varied follow-up intervals; figure/table extracted from review (xu2024theeffectivenessof media f6fdbb57, xu2024theeffectivenessof media 67ffe9ba) | Best quantitative evidence for occupational outcomes, but heterogeneity is high for sick-leave reduction (xu2024theeffectivenessof pages 21-22, xu2024theeffectivenessof media f6fdbb57) |
