snippets: Mori, Tajima, Kimura et al. 22) delivered web-based CBT to employees (168 Japanese system engineers) experiencing distress.
Participants received a 2.5-h group education CBT session in the workplace and 1 month of online CBT-based homework exercises.
The program had no significant impact on psychological distress, problem solving scores, or in the recognition of dysfunctional thinking compared with controls.

A small significant improvement versus controls was observed in the intervention group’s ability to transform thoughts postintervention (d = 0.26) and their ability to cope with stress at 6-month follow-up (d = 0.37).
Further analyses were then conducted with data from 73 participants with clinically significant levels of emotional distress at baseline.

Participants with distress in the intervention group reported significantly lower psychological distress scores postintervention compared with similar participants in the control group with medium effect size (d = 0.61). Improvement was maintained at 6-month-follow-up (d = 0.60).
A CBT intervention delivered purely via e-mail was also trialed with 177 employees from a variety of occupations 21).
Significant medium to large improvements were made by the intervention group on scores of stress, depression, anxiety, and emotional exhaustion.

The odds of recovery from clinical stress were significantly higher for participants in the intervention group compared with those in the control, with similar recovery effects found for depression and emotional exhaustion but not anxiety.

Ninety-seven participants from the intervention group were traced at a 3-year follow-up. The gains made by this group from preintervention were more pronounced, ranging from d = 1.3 for anxiety to d = 1.8 for stress.
Both groups improved in work functioning over time, with no significant differences between the two.

contexts.context: The excerpt reports that RCTs of return-to-work (RTW) interventions for work-related stress commonly included CBT components alongside problem-solving, exposure, occupational therapy, psychoeducation, diagnosis/consultation and referral. Formats included face-to-face CBT, work-focused CBT variants, and web-based/physician-delivered interventions. Comparators in these trials were typically control or care-as-usual (CAU). Work outcomes assessed across trials included return-to-work (RTW) and sick leave, with RTW measured at short (around 6 months) and longer follow-ups (12–18 months); the excerpt lists multiple trials assessing RTW at 12–18 months. The text notes that no RCTs examined pharmacotherapy for RTW. Reporting of effectiveness on RTW appears mixed in the reviewed trials (some trials assessed RTW at different time points and some reported no clear RTW effect), but specific effect sizes or consistent positive findings for CBT on both stress/burnout measures and work outcomes are not provided in this excerpt.

contexts.context: The excerpt reports results from a randomized trial of a self-guided internet- and mobile-based stress management intervention (iSMI) for employees. The intervention produced reductions in perceived stress and yielded effects that overlap with prior meta-analytic benchmarks (the cited meta-analysis reported d=1.15 for stress reduction and d=0.71 for mental health). The trial included only employees with highly elevated symptoms of stress (and mean baseline depression and anxiety >3 points above clinical cut-offs), and excluded over 120 low-stress applicants, so effects may be stronger in this highly distressed sample. The authors note a possible incremental benefit from adding a text-message coach but state that randomized comparisons of the intervention with versus without mobile/text components are needed to confirm this. Limitations noted include trial recruitment/selection bias, lack of data on negative effects, limited information on long-term comparisons, and uncertain generalisability; they recommend studies comparing acceptability, effectiveness and cost-effectiveness of guidance formats. The excerpt does not report work-specific outcomes (sick leave, return-to-work, work ability) or detailed comparator arm descriptions.

contexts.context: This randomized trial (N=182 employees, mainly healthcare/IT/education) compared a 10-week internet-based work-focused CBT (W-iCBT) to a generic internet-based CBT (iCBT) and a waitlist control (WLC). Primary outcome was burnout measured with the Shirom-Melamed Burnout Questionnaire (SMBQ). Both W-iCBT and generic iCBT produced large and significant reductions in SMBQ versus WLC at posttreatment (Cohen d=1.00 and 0.83) and moderate effects maintained at 6-month follow-up (d=0.74 each). Moderate-to-large effects were also observed on secondary health- and work-related outcomes. Importantly, only the work-focused iCBT showed significant improvements in work ability and reductions in short-term sickness absence (445 fewer days vs WLC; 324 fewer days vs iCBT). There were no significant group differences for work experience or long-term sick leave. Thus, internet-delivered CBT (both generic and work-focused) reduced stress/burnout, while adding work-focused components appeared to confer additional benefits on short-term sickness absence and self-rated work ability.

snippets: The aim of this study was to evaluate the efficacy of an internet-based cognitive behavioral intervention for stress-related disorders integrating work-related aspects (work-focused and internet-based cognitive behavioral therapy [W-iCBT]), compared with a generic internet-based cognitive behavioral therapy (iCBT) group and a waitlist control (WLC) group.

In this trial, 182 employees, mainly employed in the health care, IT, or educational sector, who fulfilled the criteria for a stress-related disorder, were randomized to a 10-week W-iCBT (n=61, 33.5%), generic iCBT (n=61, 33.5%), or WLC (n=60, 33%).

Compared with the WLC group, participants of the W-iCBT and iCBT groups showed an equal and significant reduction in the primary outcome (Shirom-Melamed Burnout Questionnaire [SMBQ]) from pretreatment to posttreatment assessment (Cohen d=1.00 and 0.83, respectively) and at the 6-month follow-up (Cohen d=0.74 and 0.74, respectively).
Significant moderate-to-large effect sizes were also found in the secondary health- and work-related outcomes.
The W-iCBT was the only group that exhibited significant effects on work ability and short-term sickness absence.
Short-term sickness absence was 445 days lower than the WLC group and 324 days lower than the iCBT intervention group.
However, no significant differences were found in terms of work experience or long-term sick leave.
The work-focused and generic iCBT interventions proved to be superior compared with the control condition in reducing chronic stress and several other mental health–related symptoms.

contexts.context: This systematic review/meta-analysis focused on RCTs of CBT interventions aimed at helping employees on sick leave return to work and improving psychological and physical outcomes. Included trials required adult workplace participants (≥18) on sick leave. Interventions were CBT-based (both cognitive and behavioural elements mandatory) and commonly included homework, stress management, relapse prevention, problem-solving and rehabilitation; delivery formats included face-to-face and online modules. Comparators varied widely (care-as-usual, no treatment, graded activity, Qigong, etc.). Primary work outcomes were return-to-work status and sick-leave days; secondary outcomes included stress, anxiety, depression, fatigue, mental illness, working ability and physical function. Overall trial findings were mixed: some RCTs reported that CBT reduced duration of MSK-related sick leave and improved physical/mental health, while other RCTs found improvements versus no treatment but no significant difference compared with care-as-usual. The review notes heterogeneity and inconsistent results likely driven by small sample sizes, homogeneous samples, and varied control conditions.

The intervention must be based on CBT with both cognitive and behavioural therapy as compulsory components in combination with common CBT techniques including homework assignment, stress management, relapsing preventions, problem-solving strategies, and rehabilita- tion.

Control: Control group can be anything including non-CBT interventions such as care-as-usual, no treatment, graded activity, Qigong that refers to a traditional Chinese mind-body exercise to improve health by relieving stress, anxiety and improving physical health, work rehabilitation, and conventional care.
Outcome: The study outcome includes whether the employee return to work or not, and the number of days for sick leave.
Participants: Participants were employees at a workplace and on sick leave or leave due to a workplace incident. All participants must be 18 years or above.

contexts.context: This randomized controlled trial randomized 82 employees on sickness leave for work-related stress to individual SMT, group SMT, or care-as-usual (CAU). The SMT intervention consisted of 12 psychologist-delivered sessions. Outcomes were burnout and distress complaints (measured at baseline, 4, 7, and 10 months) and absenteeism (measured across the study period). Across all conditions there were substantial reductions in both complaints and sickness absence between baseline and 4 months; after 4 months, complaints largely stabilized while sickness absence continued to decline. The trial found no consistent or significant advantage of individual or group SMT over CAU on either psychological (distress/burnout) outcomes or work outcomes (sickness absence/return-to-work). The authors conclude that, despite prior positive findings of CBT-based stress interventions in healthy or at-risk samples, CBT-based interventions as implemented here did not demonstrate success for patients with clinical levels of work-related stress. The study had hypothesized larger reductions with SMT versus CAU but did not find support for that hypothesis.

Individual and group cognitive-behavioral treatment for work-related stress complaints and sickness absence: a randomized controlled trial. Journal of occupational health psychology, 13 3:214-31, Jan 2008. URL: https://doi.org/10.1037/1076-8998.13.3.214, doi:10.1037/1076-8998.13.3.214. This article has 220 citations and is from a highest quality peer-reviewed journal.

snippets: Eighty-two patients on sickness leave with work-related stress were randomly assigned to (a) individual SMT, (b) group SMT, or (c) care as usual (CAU).
The SMT comprised 12 sessions conducted by a psychologist.
Complaints of burnout and distress were measured at baseline, and at 4, 7, and 10 months.
Absenteeism was measured during the whole research period.
Across treatment conditions, complaints and sickness absence reduced considerably between baseline and 4 months.
Thereafter, complaints remained approximately stable, whereas sickness absence further reduced.
Hardly any significant group difference emerged, and no consistent pattern could be discerned in favor of any treatment condition.
In conclusion, this study adds to the evidence that CBT-based interventions as currently practiced are not successful in treating patients with clinical levels of work-related stress.
Hypothesis 1: SMT results in larger reductions of distress complaints, burnout complaints, and sickness leave than CAU in a clinical sample with work-related stress.

contexts.context: This randomized controlled trial evaluated a 12-week therapist-guided internet-based CBT (ICBT) for chronic stress in a clinical sample of 100 adults diagnosed with adjustment disorder (AD) or exhaustion disorder (ED). Participants were randomized to ICBT (n=50) or a waitlist control (n=50). The primary outcome was perceived stress (PSS). Compared to the control, ICBT produced a large, significant reduction in perceived stress (d = 1.09) and moderate-to-large improvements across secondary symptom domains. These symptom improvements were maintained at 6-month follow-up. However, there was no significant between-group effect on functional impairment or work ability. The authors conclude that a relatively short ICBT is effective in reducing stress-related symptoms in this clinical sample and could increase treatment accessibility, but results need replication and further study to clarify relationships between symptom reduction and functional/work outcomes.
contexts.question: Extract key results from randomized controlled trials of CBT for work-related stress (including work-focused CBT and internet-based stress management/CBT) in working populations: intervention format, comparator, main stress/burnout outcomes, and work outcomes (sick leave/return-to-work/work ability).

snippets: Internet-Based Cognitive Behavioral Therapy for Chronic Stress: A Randomized Controlled Trial
A total of 100 adults diagnosed with AD or ED were randomly assigned to a 12-week ICBT (n = 50) or waitlist control condition (n = 50).
Primary outcome was the level of perceived stress (PSS).
Compared to the control condition, patients in the ICBT group made large and significant improvements on the PSS (d = 1.09) and moderate to large improvements in secondary symptom domains.
Effects were maintained at the 6-month follow-up.
There was no significant between-group effect on functional impairment or work ability.

contexts.context: This randomized controlled trial tested a 7-week standardized cognitive behavioural treatment for work-related stress delivered via e-mail. From 342 applicants, 239 met inclusion criteria and were randomized to immediate treatment (n=177) or a waiting-list control (n=62). Primary psychological outcomes were measured with the DASS-42 and the Emotional Exhaustion scale of the MBI-GS. Attrition was 21% (50 participants). Intention-to-treat ANCOVAs showed that the treatment group improved significantly more than the waiting-list group. Within-group effects in the treatment arm were described as large-to-moderate (report gives "0.9 (stress)>d>0.5 (anxiety)"). Between-group effect sizes were reported as ranging from "d50.6 (stress) to d50.1 (anxiety)". A 3-year follow-up suggested more pronounced effects, but high attrition limits confidence. The paper recommends further research comparing Internet-driven CBT with face-to-face care and examining optimal therapist contact. The excerpt does not report work-specific outcomes (sick leave, return-to-work, or work ability) in the presented sections.

snippets: The aim of this study was to assess the effects of a 7-week standardized cognitive behavioural treatment of work-related stress conducted via e-mail.
A total of 342 people applied for treatment in reaction to a newspaper article.
Initial screening reduced the sample to a heterogeneous (sub)clinical group of 239 participants.
Participants were assigned randomly to a waiting  list condition (n562), or to immediate treatment (n5177).
A follow-up was conducted 3 years after inception of the treatment.
The outcome measures used were the Depression Anxiety Stress Scales (DASS-42) and the Emotional Exhaustion scale of the Maslach Burnout Inventory – General Survey (MBI-GS).
Fifty participants (21%) dropped out.
Intention-to-treat analysis of covariance (ANCOVAs) revealed that participants in the treatment condition improved significantly more than those in the waiting control condition
In the treatment group, the effects were large to moderate (0.9 (stress)>d>0.5 (anxiety)).
The between-group effects ranged from d50.6 (stress) to d50.1 (anxiety).
At follow-up, the effects were more pronounced, but this result requires replication in view of high attrition at follow-up.
The results warrant further research on Internet-driven standardized cognitive behavioural therapy for work-related stress.

contexts.context: This RCT evaluated a self-guided internet- and mobile-supported stress management intervention (iSMI) for employees (N=264; PSS-10 ≥22). The intervention comprised seven sessions plus one booster, focusing on problem-solving and emotion regulation. Participants were randomized to the iSMI or a 6-month wait-list control with treatment as usual. Primary outcome was perceived stress (PSS-10), assessed at baseline, 7 weeks (post-treatment) and 6 months. The iSMI produced large short-term reductions in perceived stress (7 weeks d=0.96, 95% CI 0.70–1.21) and sustained moderate reductions at 6 months (d=0.65, 95% CI 0.40–0.89) versus control. Secondary outcomes (depression, anxiety, emotional exhaustion, sleep problems, worry, mental health–related QoL, psychological detachment, emotion regulation skills) also improved with small-to-moderate effect sizes. Presenteeism improved, but at 6 months there were no significant differences for work engagement, physical health–related QoL, or absenteeism. Overall, the self-guided internet CBT-style program was effective for reducing perceived stress and several related mental and some work outcomes, but did not significantly lower absenteeism at 6 months.

snippets: A sample of 264 employees with elevated symptoms of perceived stress (Perceived Stress Scale, PSS-10 ≥22) was randomly assigned to either the iSMI or to the WLC.
The iSMI consisted of seven sessions and one booster session including problem-solving and emotion regulation techniques.
The primary outcome was perceived stress (PSS-10).

The iSMI participants showed a significantly higher reduction in perceived stress from baseline to post-treatment at 7 weeks (d=0.96, 95% CI 0.70 to 1.21) and to the 6-month follow-up (d=0.65, 95% CI 0.40 to 0.89) compared to the WLC.

Significant differences with small to moderate effect sizes were also found for depression, anxiety, emotional exhaustion, sleeping problems, worrying, mental health-related quality of life, psychological detachment, emotion regulation skills and presenteeism, in favour of the experimental group.

At the 6 -month follow-up, all outcomes remained signiﬁcantly better for the experimental group with the exception of work engagement, physical health-related quality of life and absenteeism, which were not found to signiﬁcantly differ between the iSMI and WLC groups.
Conclusions The iSMI investigated in this study was found to be effective in reducing typical mental-related and work-related health symptoms of stressed employees.

contexts.context: The excerpt reports results from a meta-analysis of CBT-based interventions for employees on sick leave. Overall the authors state CBT "significantly increased the number of people who RTW and improved physical function," though no RCT-only RTW/sick-leave numeric estimate is provided in the excerpt. Depression outcomes (15 studies, n=2870) showed a mean reduction of −0.965 (95% CI −1.745 to −0.186; p<0.05) and a small standardized effect size of −0.176 (95% CI −0.322 to −0.031; p<0.05). Another reported mean reduction (unspecified outcome in excerpt) was −0.810 (95% CI −1.561 to −0.060; p<0.05) with SMD −0.196 (95% CI −0.308 to −0.083; p<0.01). Subgroup data for RCTs (13 RCTs, n≈2676) show small but significant post–pre depression effects (reported in table as MD ≈ −0.175 and SMD ≈ −0.154). Publication-bias testing (Egger) was non-significant for sick-leave reduced days (p=0.098) and overall minimal (p>0.05). The excerpt provides clear quantitative evidence of small improvements in depression/anxiety symptoms and a qualitative statement of increased RTW, but it lacks RCT-specific numeric estimates for RTW/sick-leave and for burnout/exhaustion or stress outcomes.
contexts.question: In RCTs of workplace/work-related stress, what were the effects of CBT on sickness absence/return-to-work and symptom outcomes (stress, burnout/exhaustion, depression/anxiety)? Extract any quantitative results if available.

contexts.context: This randomized trial compared a work-focused internet CBT (W-iCBT), a generic iCBT, and a waitlist control in employees with stress-related disorders. Both W-iCBT and generic iCBT produced large reductions in burnout (primary outcome, measured by SMBQ): posttreatment Cohen's d = 1.00 (W-iCBT) and 0.83 (iCBT), and at 6 months both d = 0.74. Secondary health- and work-related outcomes showed moderate-to-large effects overall. Importantly, only the work-focused intervention (W-iCBT) produced significant improvements in work ability and reduced short-term sickness absence: 445 fewer short-term sickness absence days versus waitlist and 324 fewer days versus generic iCBT. There were no significant differences in work experience or long-term sick leave. The excerpt does not provide separate quantitative results for depression or anxiety outcomes beyond the general statement about secondary health outcomes, so symptom-specific effect sizes for depression/anxiety are not available here.
contexts.question: In RCTs of workplace/work-related stress, what were the effects of CBT on sickness absence/return-to-work and symptom outcomes (stress, burnout/exhaustion, depression/anxiety)? Extract any quantitative results if available.

snippets: Compared with the WLC group, participants of the W-iCBT and iCBT groups showed an equal and significant reduction in the primary outcome (Shirom-Melamed Burnout Questionnaire [SMBQ]) from pretreatment to posttreatment assessment (Cohen d=1.00 and 0.83, respectively) and at the 6-month follow-up (Cohen d=0.74 and 0.74, respectively).
Significant moderate-to-large effect sizes were also found in the secondary health- and work-related outcomes.
The W-iCBT was the only group that exhibited significant effects on work ability and short-term sickness absence.
Short-term sickness absence was 445 days lower than the WLC group and 324 days lower than the iCBT intervention group.
However, no significant differences were found in terms of work experience or long-term sick leave.

contexts.context: This randomized trial (N = 211; CBT n=64, RTW-I n=67, COMBO n=80) analyzed a Stress subgroup (adjustment or exhaustion disorder; n = 152) with primary outcomes of Clinician Severity Rating (CSR) and registry sick-leave data. Subgroup analyses found that, for stress-related disorders, CBT produced greater symptom reduction than the return-to-work intervention (RTW-I) at posttreatment, while the combined intervention (COMBO) did not differ from either CBT or RTW-I. Despite symptom benefits, there were no differences between treatments on sick-leave outcomes within the Stress subgroup. An exploratory subgroup (patients with depression, anxiety or insomnia) suggested RTW-I reduced sick leave faster than CBT. The authors conclude CBT may be promising for stress and exhaustion disorder, but note prior meta-analyses have generally found no clear treatment effects for clinical stress/exhaustion and only small effects of psychological interventions on sick leave for common mental disorders. The excerpt does not provide numerical effect sizes or statistical estimates for symptom or sick-leave outcomes.
contexts.question: In RCTs of workplace/work-related stress, what were the effects of CBT on sickness absence/return-to-work and symptom outcomes (stress, burnout/exhaustion, depression/anxiety)? Extract any quantitative results if available.

snippets: The aim of this study was to evaluate speciﬁc effects for patients with adjustment or exhaustion disorder, the Stress subgroup (n = 152), regarding symptom severity and sick leave after CBT, a return-to-work intervention (RTW-I), and a combination of them (COMBO), using data from a randomized trial.
primary care patients on sick leave (N = 211) were randomized to CBT (n = 64), RTW-I (n = 67), or COMBO (n = 80).
Blinded Clinician Severity Rating (CSR) of symptoms and sick leave registry data were primary outcomes.
Subgroup analyses showed that for the Stress subgroup, CBT led to greater reduction of symptoms than RTW-I posttreatment, but COMBO did not differ from CBT or RTW-I.
Regarding sick leave, there was no difference between treatments in the Stress subgroup.
An exploratory analysis of the treatment effects in a subgroup of patients with depression, anxiety or insomnia indicates that RTW-I reduced sick leave faster than CBT.
We conclude that CBT may be promising as an effective treatment of stress and exhaustion disorder.

For patients with clinical levels of stress and exhaustion, meta-analyses have shown no effect of treatments (Ahola, Toppinen-Tanner, & Sepp€anen, 2017; Arends, Bruinvels, Rebergen et al., 2012; Perski, Grossi, Perski, & Niemi, 2017), although some studies indicated positive effects (i.e., Linds€ater, Salomonsson, Santoft et al., 2018; Persson Asplund, Dag€o€o, Fjellstr€om et al., 2017).

Meta-analyses show that psychological interventions have a small but signiﬁcant effect in reducing sick leave compared to care as usual (CAU) for individuals with CMDs (Nigatu, Liu, Uppal et al., 2016; Salomonsson, Hedman-Lagerl€of, & €Ost, 2018a).

contexts.context: This RCT of a guided internet-based stress management intervention (iSMI) for distressed managers found substantial reductions in perceived stress versus an attention control (AC). PSS-14 means (iSMI vs AC) were: pretreatment T1 33.22 vs 33.09; post-treatment T2 24.39 vs 29.11; 6-month T3 22.52 vs 26.61, indicating larger and sustained symptom improvement in the iSMI group. The authors report effects within the range of guided interventions (Cohen’s d=0.64, 95% CI 0.50–0.79). They note that compared with regular face-to-face CBT-based stress management interventions (SMIs; d=0.68–1.16), their effects are lower on stress-related measures (perceived stress d=0.74; burnout d=0.95) but comparable on anxiety and depression outcomes. Work-related outcomes were not sustained at 6 months, and the AC group showed continued improvement reducing between-group differences at follow-up. No data on sickness absence or return-to-work are reported in this excerpt.
contexts.question: In RCTs of workplace/work-related stress, what were the effects of CBT on sickness absence/return-to-work and symptom outcomes (stress, burnout/exhaustion, depression/anxiety)? Extract any quantitative results if available.

snippets: Results from the present study add to the growing body of studies showing that iSMIs can be an accessible and effective way    of reducing work-related stress.
The effects found in the present study were larger compared
Compared with regular face-to-face CBT-based SMIs, d=0.68 to 1.16,17 our effects are lower on

work-related outcomes were not sustained at the 6 months follow-up.

contexts.context: This randomized trial (N=264; PSS-10 ≥22) evaluated a self-guided internet/mobile stress-management intervention (iSMI: seven sessions + booster, including problem-solving and emotion-regulation techniques) versus a 6-month wait-list control. The primary outcome was perceived stress (PSS-10). The iSMI produced a large reduction in perceived stress at post-treatment (7 weeks) with d=0.96 (95% CI 0.70–1.21) and a moderate effect at 6 months, d=0.65 (95% CI 0.40–0.89). Secondary outcomes showed significant small-to-moderate improvements in depression, anxiety, emotional exhaustion (burnout-related), sleep problems, worrying, mental health–related quality of life, psychological detachment, emotion-regulation skills, and presenteeism in favor of the iSMI. However, at 6 months, absenteeism (sickness absence) did not differ significantly between groups, nor did work engagement or physical health–related quality of life. In sum, this trial shows meaningful symptom improvements (stress, burnout-related emotional exhaustion, depression/anxiety) but no detected effect on absenteeism at 6 months.
contexts.question: In RCTs of workplace/work-related stress, what were the effects of CBT on sickness absence/return-to-work and symptom outcomes (stress, burnout/exhaustion, depression/anxiety)? Extract any quantitative results if available.

snippets: A sample of 264 employees with elevated symptoms of perceived stress (Perceived Stress Scale, PSS-10 ≥22) was randomly assigned to either the iSMI or to the WLC.
The iSMI consisted of seven sessions and one booster session including problem-solving and emotion regulation techniques.
The primary outcome was perceived stress (PSS-10).

The iSMI participants showed a significantly higher reduction in perceived stress from baseline to post-treatment at 7 weeks (d=0.96, 95% CI 0.70 to 1.21) and to the 6-month follow-up (d=0.65, 95% CI 0.40 to 0.89) compared to the WLC.

Significant differences with small to moderate effect sizes were also found for depression, anxiety, emotional exhaustion, sleeping problems, worrying, mental health-related quality of life, psychological detachment, emotion regulation skills and presenteeism, in favour of the experimental group.

At the 6 -month follow-up, all outcomes remained signiﬁcantly better for the experimental group with the exception of work engagement, physical health-related quality of life and absenteeism, which were not found to signiﬁcantly differ between the iSMI and WLC groups.

contexts.context: This randomized controlled trial tested a 7-week e-mailed standardized CBT for work-related stress (n=177 immediate treatment; n=62 wait-list; initial applicants 342, screened to 239). Outcomes were measured with the DASS-42 and the Emotional Exhaustion scale of the MBI-GS. Intention-to-treat ANCOVAs showed the treatment group improved significantly more than the waiting list. Within the treatment group effects on symptoms were reported as large-to-moderate (reported as 0.9 for stress down to ~0.5 for anxiety). Between-group effect sizes were smaller: reported as ranging from d=0.6 for stress to d=0.1 for anxiety. Dropout was 21% (50 participants). A 3-year follow-up indicated more pronounced effects, but the authors caution interpretation due to high attrition. The excerpt does not report sickness absence or return-to-work outcomes, so no quantitative RTW/sick-leave effects are available here.
contexts.question: In RCTs of workplace/work-related stress, what were the effects of CBT on sickness absence/return-to-work and symptom outcomes (stress, burnout/exhaustion, depression/anxiety)? Extract any quantitative results if available.

snippets: The outcome measures used were the Depression Anxiety Stress Scales (DASS-42) and the Emotional Exhaustion scale of the Maslach Burnout Inventory – General Survey (MBI-GS).
A total of 342 people applied for treatment in reaction to a newspaper article.
Initial screening reduced the sample to a heterogeneous (sub)clinical group of 239 participants.
Participants were assigned randomly to a waiting list condition (n562), or to immediate treatment (n5177).
Fifty participants (21%) dropped out.
Intention-to-treat analysis of covariance (ANCOVAs) revealed that participants in the treatment condition improved significantly more than those in the waiting control condition
In the treatment group, the effects were large to moderate (0.9 (stress)>d>0.5 (anxiety)).
The between-group effects ranged from d50.6 (stress) to d50.1 (anxiety).
At follow-up, the effects were more pronounced, but this result requires replication in view of high attrition at follow-up.

contexts.context: This systematic review/meta-analysis pooled 34 RCTs of CBT-based interventions for employees on sick leave. Fifteen RCTs (n=1727) reported sickness absence and found a mean reduction in sick-leave days of −3.654 (95% CI −5.253 to −2.046; p<0.001). Sixteen papers (n=2298) reported return-to-work timing, with the intervention group returning about 1.5 days earlier (95% CI 1.019 to 1.722; p<0.05). Symptom outcomes: the authors report CBT-based interventions were effective for fatigue, mental illness, and depression and improved physical function, but showed no effects for stress, anxiety, or working ability. The review targeted studies of employees on sick leave for causes including depression, stress-related mental disorders, and chronic pain; the excerpt does not isolate results exclusively from RCTs of workplace/work-related stress, so direct applicability to workplace-specific stress trials is limited in this passage.
contexts.question: In RCTs of workplace/work-related stress, what were the effects of CBT on sickness absence/return-to-work and symptom outcomes (stress, burnout/exhaustion, depression/anxiety)? Extract any quantitative results if available.

snippets: Thirty-four RCTs were included in the analysis.
Fifteen RCTs with 1727 participants reported on sick leave.
participants who completed CBT intervention had reduced sick leave in days (mean reduction − 3.654; 95%CI − 5.253, − 2.046; p < 0.001)
Sixteen papers with 2298 participants reported that the intervention group RTW 1.5 days earlier (95%CI 1.019, 1.722; p<0.05).
CBT-based interventions were effective in managing fatigue, mental illness, and depression, and improving physical function while it showed no effects in managing stress, anxiety and working ability.
This study compiles research on utilizing CBT-based interventions for helping employees on sick leave return to work.

contexts.context: In the included RCTs of CBT for work-related/sick-leave populations, CBT reduced sickness absence days and increased return-to-work. Across 15 studies (n=1727) CBT reduced sick-leave days by a mean of −3.649 (95% CI −5.253 to −2.046; p<0.001) with a moderate effect size (−0.395). Across 16 studies (n=2298) CBT increased number returning to work (mean difference 1.5, 95% CI 1.019–1.722; p<0.05). Heterogeneity was high for sick-leave days (I2=92.991) but low/non-significant for RTW (I2=32.998). Symptom outcomes categorized as “mental illness” (5 studies, n=1301) showed a small improvement (MD −0.810) and a small effect size (−0.196) per Table 2. Subgroup analyses found larger sick-leave reductions when interventions were face-to-face (MD −8.673), included stress-management components (MD −3.498), used homework (MD −2.615), mood-management (effect size −0.926), longer courses (≥16 weeks) or longer sessions (≥90 min), combined CBT (MD −14.785), or group delivery. The excerpt does not separately report burnout/exhaustion or detailed depression/anxiety-specific estimates beyond the “mental illness” category.
contexts.question: In RCTs of workplace/work-related stress, what were the effects of CBT on sickness absence/return-to-work and symptom outcomes (stress, burnout/exhaustion, depression/anxiety)? Extract any quantitative results if available.

snippets: Out of the 34 papers, 15 papers with 1727 participants investigated on reduction of sick leave, and the results showed that CBT-based intervention was more effective than the control condition in reducing sick leave in days with a mean reduction of − 3.649 (95% Cl − 5.253 to − 2.046; p < 0.001) and a moderate effect size of − 0.395 (95% Cl − 0.670 to − 0.120; p < 0.01).

Sixteen papers with 2298 participants reported on the number of people RTW. The results showed that CBT-based intervention was more effective than the control condition in facilitating people to RTW with a mean difference of 1.5 (95% Cl 1.019  − 1.722; p < 0.05).
The heterogeneity of these studies was statistically significant ­(I2 = 92.991; p < 0.001) (Table2).
The heterogeneity of these studies was not statistically significant ­(I2 = 32.998, p > 0.05).
Table 2   Result of all variable analysis of included studies in meta-analysis

Table 3 suggested that CBT based intervention in the studies with the following characteristics had a significant effect on sick leave in reduced day: treatment delivered face-to-face (MD − 8.673, 95% CI − 15.550, − 1.797, p < 0.05), participants with higher education levels (> 12 years) (effect size − 0.923, 95% CI − 1.206, − 0.639, p < 0.001), utilization of rehabilitation services (MD − 10.095, 95% CI − 11.902, − 8.288, p < 0.001), stress management (MD − 3.498, 95% CI − 5.110, − 1.886, p < 0.001), and long treatment course (≥ 16 weeks) (MD 2.747, 95% CI − 4.169, − 1.326, p < 0.001).

CBT-based intervention showed a better effect on sick leave in studies that included homework assignment (MD − 2.615, 95% CI − 4.017, − 1.213, p < 0.001), mood management (effect size − 0.926, 95% CI − 1.209, − 0.643), long duration of sessions (≥ 90 min) (MD − 9.951, 95% CI − 17.633, − 2.269, p < 0.05), combined CBT (MD − 14.785, 95% CI − 22.898, − 6.672, p < 0.001) and those delivered in group sessions (effect size − 9.476, 95% CI − 11.247, − 7.704, p < 0.001).

contexts.context: The excerpt summarizes a systematic review/meta-analysis protocol assessing RCTs of CBT for employees on sick leave, focusing on return-to-work (RTW) and days of sick leave as primary outcomes and symptoms (stress, anxiety, depression, fatigue, working ability, physical function) as secondary outcomes. Inclusion required RCTs of CBT with both cognitive and behavioral components in adult employees on workplace-related sick leave. The text reports mixed trial findings: some RCTs showed CBT significantly reduced duration of musculoskeletal (MSK)-related sick leaves and improved physical and mental health, and related interventions also showed benefits for RTW after mental-condition leaves (stress, burnout). However, other RCTs found no significant difference between CBT and care-as-usual despite improvements versus no-treatment controls. The excerpt does not provide quantitative effect sizes, confidence intervals, or pooled estimates — only qualitative statements about significance and inconsistency across studies. Searches covered multiple databases for studies from 1990 to mid-2022.
contexts.question: In RCTs of workplace/work-related stress, what were the effects of CBT on sickness absence/return-to-work and symptom outcomes (stress, burnout/exhaustion, depression/anxiety)? Extract any quantitative results if available.

snippets: The effectiveness of CBT in reducing the duration of sick leave has been investigated in various RCTs across different countries, albeit with inconsistent results.

Previous RCTs have shown that CBT-based treatment can significantly reduce the length of MSK-related sick leaves and is effective in improving the physical and mental health of employees [20, 21, 29].

Other similar interventions have also been shown to have a significant effect on helping employees return to work after taking sick leaves due to mental conditions including stress and burnout [30, 31].

However, results from various studies are inconsistent with some RCTs showing that although CBT-based treatment led to improved outcomes compared to no treatment, there was no significant difference between the proposed treatment and care as usual [32–35].
Outcome: The study outcome includes whether the employee return to work or not, and the number of days for sick leave. The secondary outcomes included stress,
Participants: Participants were employees at a workplace and on sick leave or leave due to a workplace incident. All participants must be 18 years or above.
Intervention: The study design was RCT or RCT equivalent studies. The intervention must be based on CBT with both cognitive and behavioural therapy as compulsory components
Searches were confined to literature published from January 1, 1990 to June 27th 2022

contexts.context: Across included reviews of workplace interventions, CBT shows consistent symptom reduction for common mental disorders (rated highly: "Symptom reduction: ⋆⋆⋆"). Reviews report significant improvements in anxiety and depression symptomology and promising effects as an early secondary intervention for reducing work-related stress. However, improvements in clinical symptoms did not reliably translate into work-related outcomes in general workplace stress trials (e.g. "this did not translate to notable improvements in work-related factors such as absenteeism and productivity"). Where occupational outcomes improve, it is typically when CBT is delivered as part of a return-to-work (RTW) programme or when therapy is explicitly work-focused (e.g. problem-focused CBT). One review reported a quantitative RTW benefit: CBT may facilitate an earlier RTW by an average of 2 weeks among employees with adjustment disorder. Overall, the evidence base is mixed in quality; some reviews concluded that study quality was low and prevented firm recommendations about organizational outcomes.
contexts.question: In RCTs of workplace/work-related stress, what were the effects of CBT on sickness absence/return-to-work and symptom outcomes (stress, burnout/exhaustion, depression/anxiety)? Extract any quantitative results if available.

Taken together, these results suggest that CBT can result in signiﬁcant improvements in anxiety/depression symptomology.

While there is evidence that CBT can improve occupational outcomes, this appears to be dependent on therapy focusing directly on work matters, such as when part of a formal RTW programme or when emphasizing the development of problem-solving skills directly related to the workplace.

answer.formatted_answer:
Question: What does peer-reviewed literature say about the effectiveness of cognitive behavioural therapy for workplace stress?

## Technical summary

Peer-reviewed evidence indicates that cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT)–based interventions can reduce *work-related stress symptoms* (perceived stress, burnout/exhaustion and related distress) in employees, with effects often in the small-to-moderate range on average but sometimes large in specific internet-delivered programs and higher-symptom samples. Evidence that CBT reliably improves *work outcomes* (absenteeism/sick leave, return-to-work [RTW], work ability) is weaker and more variable, with better occupational effects when CBT is explicitly work-focused or embedded in RTW-oriented programs. (joyce2016workplaceinterventionsfor pages 8-9, bhui2012asynthesisof pages 1-2, richardson2008effectsofoccupational pages 14-15)

| Study | Study type / population | CBT format | Main outcomes | Quantitative effect | Durability / follow-up | Notes on work outcomes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Richardson & Rothstein 2008 | Meta-analysis of occupational stress-management interventions in workers | CBT, multimodal, relaxation, organizational programs | Overall stress/mental-health outcomes | Overall pooled effect d≈0.53; high heterogeneity (I²≈73%); few absenteeism/productivity studies (richardson2008effectsofoccupational pages 14-15) | Not summarized as a single follow-up estimate in excerpt | Supports moderate overall benefit of stress-management interventions, but evidence on absenteeism/productivity is sparse (richardson2008effectsofoccupational pages 14-15) |
| van der Klink / Klink et al. 2001 | Meta-analysis of 48 experimental studies of work-related stress interventions | Cognitive-behavioural, multimodal, relaxation, organizational | Complaints, psychological resources/responses, quality of work life | CBT and multimodal interventions showed moderate effects; organizational interventions non-significant; excerpt does not provide exact pooled d by CBT subgroup (klink2001thebenefitsof pages 1-2) | Not specified in excerpt | Stronger evidence for individual than organizational outcomes; work-outcome estimates not detailed (klink2001thebenefitsof pages 1-2) |
| Bhui et al. 2012 | Review of 23 systematic reviews / 499 primary studies | Individual interventions including CBT | Anxiety, depression, absenteeism | CBT programs repeatedly showed larger individual-level mental-health effects than other approaches; no numeric pooled CBT estimate in excerpt (bhui2012asynthesisof pages 1-2) | Review-level synthesis; no single durability estimate | Evidence for absenteeism is limited; organizational interventions mixed (bhui2012asynthesisof pages 1-2) |
| Joyce et al. 2016 | Systematic meta-review of workplace interventions for common mental disorders | CBT-based workplace and RTW-focused interventions | Symptom reduction, absenteeism, productivity, RTW | Consistent symptom reduction; one cited Cochrane review suggested earlier RTW by ~2 weeks; occupational effects otherwise mixed (joyce2016workplaceinterventionsfor pages 8-9) | Depends on underlying reviews | Work-focused / RTW-oriented CBT appears more likely to improve occupational outcomes than generic CBT (joyce2016workplaceinterventionsfor pages 8-9) |
| Ryan et al. 2017 | Systematic review of web-based workplace stress interventions | Web-based CBT / iCBT / blended CBT | Stress, mental health, insomnia, work functioning | Across trials, effects ranged from about d=0.3 to >1.0; examples include perceived stress d=0.96 post and d=0.65 at 6 months in GET.ON Stress-related trials (ryan2017webbasedinterventionsfor pages 3-5) | Some studies reported 6-month maintenance; uncontrolled 3-year follow-up in one trial (ryan2017webbasedinterventionsfor pages 3-5, ryan2017webbasedinterventionsfor pages 5-6) | Work effects inconsistent; many studies found no absenteeism/presenteeism benefit, though some showed faster RTW or small work-performance gains (ryan2017webbasedinterventionsfor pages 3-5, ryan2017webbasedinterventionsfor pages 6-7) |
| Xu et al. 2024 | Systematic review and meta-analysis of 34 RCTs in employees on sick leave | CBT-based interventions, including stress-management and RTW-oriented variants | Sick leave, RTW, depression, stress/anxiety, work ability | Sick leave reduced by MD −3.654 days (95% CI −5.253 to −2.046); RTW ~1.5 days earlier; depression small benefit (effect sizes ~−0.176 to −0.196); no pooled effect for stress, anxiety, or working ability (xu2024theeffectivenessof pages 1-2, xu2024theeffectivenessof pages 27-28, xu2024theeffectivenessof pages 21-22, xu2024theeffectivenessof media f6fdbb57) | Pooled across varied follow-up intervals; figure/table extracted from review (xu2024theeffectivenessof media f6fdbb57, xu2024theeffectivenessof media 67ffe9ba) | Best quantitative evidence for occupational outcomes, but heterogeneity is high for sick-leave reduction (xu2024theeffectivenessof pages 21-22, xu2024theeffectivenessof media f6fdbb57) |
| Ebert 2016 | RCT in employees with elevated perceived stress (N=264) | Self-guided internet/mobile CBT-based stress management | Perceived stress, depression/anxiety, emotional exhaustion, presenteeism, absenteeism | Perceived stress d=0.96 post, d=0.65 at 6 months; secondary outcomes improved with small-to-moderate effects (ebert2016selfguidedinternetbasedand pages 1-2) | 6-month follow-up with maintained moderate stress benefit (ebert2016selfguidedinternetbasedand pages 1-2) | Improved presenteeism, but no significant absenteeism effect at 6 months (ebert2016selfguidedinternetbasedand pages 1-2) |
| Lindsäter 2018 | RCT in clinical stress-related disorders / adjustment or exhaustion disorder (N=100) | Therapist-guided internet CBT | Perceived stress, secondary stress-related symptoms, work ability | Large stress reduction vs waitlist, d=1.09; moderate-to-large secondary symptom improvements (lindsater2018internetbasedcognitivebehavioral pages 1-2) | Effects maintained at 6 months (lindsater2018internetbasedcognitivebehavioral pages 1-2) | No significant effect on work ability or functional impairment (lindsater2018internetbasedcognitivebehavioral pages 1-2) |
| Ruwaard 2007 | RCT in adults with work-related stress (n=239 randomized) | 7-week e-mailed standardized CBT | Stress, depression, anxiety, emotional exhaustion | Within-group effects ~d=0.5–0.9; between-group effects ~d=0.1–0.6, largest for stress (ruwaard2007e‐mailedstandardizedcognitive pages 1-4) | Follow-up suggested more pronounced effects, but attrition limited interpretation (ruwaard2007e‐mailedstandardizedcognitive pages 1-4) | No work-specific outcomes reported in extracted sections (ruwaard2007e‐mailedstandardizedcognitive pages 1-4) |
| Asplund 2023 | RCT in employees with stress-related disorders (N=182) | Work-focused iCBT vs generic iCBT vs waitlist | Burnout, work ability, sickness absence | Burnout post: d=1.00 (work-focused) and 0.83 (generic); both d=0.74 at 6 months; only work-focused iCBT improved work ability and reduced short-term sickness absence (445 fewer days vs waitlist; 324 fewer vs generic iCBT) (asplund2023workfocusedversusgeneric pages 1-2) | 6-month benefits maintained; 12-month assessment mentioned in trial (asplund2023workfocusedversusgeneric pages 1-2) | Strong evidence that adding work-focused components may improve short-term occupational outcomes beyond symptom relief (asplund2023workfocusedversusgeneric pages 1-2) |
| Salomonsson 2020 | Randomized trial in primary-care patients on sick leave; stress subgroup with adjustment/exhaustion disorder (n=152 within larger N=211 trial) | Face-to-face CBT vs RTW intervention vs combined | Symptom severity, registry sick leave | CBT reduced symptoms more than RTW intervention posttreatment; no numeric effect size in excerpt; no sick-leave difference between groups (salomonsson2020effectsofcognitive pages 1-2) | Posttreatment and registry follow-up reported in trial, but no compact numeric durability estimate in excerpt | Illustrates common pattern: symptom benefit without clear added sick-leave benefit for CBT alone (salomonsson2020effectsofcognitive pages 1-2) |


*Table: This table summarizes major peer-reviewed reviews and randomized trials on CBT for workplace or work-related stress. It highlights that CBT generally improves stress and related symptoms, while work outcomes such as absenteeism and return-to-work improve less consistently unless interventions are explicitly work-focused.*

## 1) Effectiveness for stress and burnout/exhaustion symptoms

### Evidence from workplace stress meta-analyses and meta-reviews

Meta-analytic and “review of reviews” evidence consistently places CBT-style programs among the more effective *individual-level* approaches for improving mental health outcomes in the context of work stress. A large meta-analysis of occupational stress management interventions reported an overall moderate pooled effect (d≈0.53), but with substantial heterogeneity and limited data on objective work outcomes. (richardson2008effectsofoccupational pages 14-15)

An earlier meta-analysis similarly concluded that cognitive–behavioural and multimodal programs show stronger effects than relaxation-only and organizational-level interventions (organizational interventions often non-significant), supporting CBT as one of the more effective individual-level strategies for work-related stress reduction. (klink2001thebenefitsof pages 1-2)

A meta-review of workplace interventions for common mental disorders (CMDs) concluded that CBT-based interventions produce robust symptom reduction, including in work-related stress presentations, but also emphasized that occupational outcomes are less consistently improved. (joyce2016workplaceinterventionsfor pages 8-9, joyce2016workplaceinterventionsfor pages 1-2)

### Evidence from trials of internet-delivered CBT/stress-management programs in employees

Multiple RCTs of CBT-based internet stress management show moderate-to-large between-group effects on perceived stress. For example, a self-guided internet/mobile stress-management intervention in employees with elevated stress produced a large reduction in perceived stress at post-treatment (d=0.96) and a sustained moderate effect at 6 months (d=0.65), with additional improvements in emotional exhaustion and depression/anxiety outcomes. (ebert2016selfguidedinternetbasedand pages 1-2)

In a clinical sample with stress-related disorders (adjustment disorder or exhaustion disorder), therapist-guided iCBT produced a large reduction in perceived stress versus waitlist (d=1.09), maintained at 6 months, indicating clinically meaningful symptom improvement in more severe, diagnosed stress conditions. (lindsater2018internetbasedcognitivebehavioral pages 1-2)

In employed samples with stress-related disorders, internet-based CBT can also produce large reductions in burnout/exhaustion. In a three-arm RCT, both work-focused and generic iCBT reduced burnout strongly versus waitlist (post-treatment d=1.00 and d=0.83), with effects maintained at 6 months (both d=0.74). (asplund2023workfocusedversusgeneric pages 1-2)

## 2) Effects on work outcomes (absenteeism/sick leave, RTW, work ability)

### Work outcomes in occupational stress trials: mixed and often null

Across workplace CBT/interventions reviews, symptom reductions often do *not* translate into consistent improvements in absenteeism or productivity; occupational benefits are more likely when CBT is work-focused or delivered within RTW programs. (joyce2016workplaceinterventionsfor pages 8-9)

Consistent with this, in the employee iSMI RCT, presenteeism improved but absenteeism did not differ at 6 months, despite robust stress symptom reductions. (ebert2016selfguidedinternetbasedand pages 1-2)

In the clinical stress-disorder iCBT trial, symptom reductions did not correspond to measurable improvements in work ability or functional impairment. (lindsater2018internetbasedcognitivebehavioral pages 1-2)

In sick-listed patients with adjustment/exhaustion disorder (stress subgroup), CBT reduced symptoms more than a RTW intervention at post-treatment, but there was no between-group difference in registry-based sick leave. (salomonsson2020effectsofcognitive pages 1-2)

### Work-focused CBT may yield better occupational effects

Work-focused elements appear to be a key moderator for occupational outcomes. In the RCT comparing work-focused iCBT vs generic iCBT vs waitlist, only the work-focused iCBT group showed significant effects on work ability and short-term sickness absence (hundreds of days fewer short-term sickness absence days than controls), despite both CBT arms showing similar burnout symptom improvements. (asplund2023workfocusedversusgeneric pages 1-2)

### Pooled evidence in sick-listed workers: small but significant RTW/absence benefits

A 2024 systematic review and meta-analysis of 34 RCTs of CBT-based interventions in employees on sick leave found statistically significant but modest pooled improvements on occupational endpoints: 

- **Reduced sick leave days**: mean reduction ≈ −3.65 days (95% CI −5.25 to −2.05), with high heterogeneity. (xu2024theeffectivenessof pages 1-2, xu2024theeffectivenessof pages 21-22)
- **Earlier RTW**: ~1.5 days earlier RTW (as reported in the review’s results and forest plot/table). (xu2024theeffectivenessof pages 1-2, xu2024theeffectivenessof media f6fdbb57)

The review also found small improvements in depression outcomes but reported no pooled effects for stress, anxiety, or working ability in that sick-leave–focused evidence base. (xu2024theeffectivenessof pages 1-2, xu2024theeffectivenessof pages 27-28)

Figure/table evidence supporting these pooled work-outcome findings is visible in the extracted forest plot and summary table. (xu2024theeffectivenessof media f6fdbb57, xu2024theeffectivenessof media 67ffe9ba)

## 3) Key moderators and implementation-relevant patterns

1. **Work-focus matters for work outcomes**: Reviews and RCTs converge on the pattern that CBT reliably improves symptoms, while effects on work outcomes are stronger when interventions include explicit work-directed components (work ability, workplace contact, RTW planning, work-problem solving). (joyce2016workplaceinterventionsfor pages 8-9, asplund2023workfocusedversusgeneric pages 1-2)
2. **Baseline severity/clinical distress may moderate effects**: Web-based workplace CBT trials show larger effects in clinically distressed subgroups than in unselected worker samples. (ryan2017webbasedinterventionsfor pages 5-6)
3. **Durability**: Several internet-delivered CBT/stress-management trials report maintenance of symptom benefits through at least 6 months. (ebert2016selfguidedinternetbasedand pages 1-2, lindsater2018internetbasedcognitivebehavioral pages 1-2)

## 4) Limitations of the evidence base

- **High heterogeneity** across workplace stress intervention studies and across CBT intervention variants limits generalizability of any single pooled effect estimate. (richardson2008effectsofoccupational pages 14-15, xu2024theeffectivenessof pages 21-22)
- **Work outcomes under-measured**: classic occupational stress meta-analyses note relatively few studies measuring absenteeism/productivity, and many web-based stress trials report no objective work endpoints. (richardson2008effectsofoccupational pages 14-15, ryan2017webbasedinterventionsfor pages 3-5)
- **Comparator effects and natural recovery**: in sick-listed stress populations, symptom improvement may occur in multiple arms over time, reducing detectable between-group differences in sick leave. (salomonsson2020effectsofcognitive pages 1-2)

## Conclusion

Peer-reviewed literature supports CBT (including internet-delivered CBT and CBT-based stress management) as an effective approach for reducing workplace stress symptoms and burnout-related outcomes, with moderate average effects and several trials reporting large symptom improvements sustained for months. (klink2001thebenefitsof pages 1-2, richardson2008effectsofoccupational pages 14-15, ebert2016selfguidedinternetbasedand pages 1-2, lindsater2018internetbasedcognitivebehavioral pages 1-2)

However, translating symptom improvement into reduced absenteeism and improved work functioning is less consistent. The strongest evidence for occupational endpoints comes from sick-leave/RTW-focused CBT trials pooled in meta-analysis (showing small but significant reductions in sick-leave days and slightly earlier RTW), and from trials where CBT is explicitly *work-focused*, which appear more likely to improve work ability and short-term sickness absence. (xu2024theeffectivenessof pages 21-22, asplund2023workfocusedversusgeneric pages 1-2, xu2024theeffectivenessof media f6fdbb57)

artifacts.artifact-00:
| Study | Study type / population | CBT format | Main outcomes | Quantitative effect | Durability / follow-up | Notes on work outcomes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Richardson & Rothstein 2008 | Meta-analysis of occupational stress-management interventions in workers | CBT, multimodal, relaxation, organizational programs | Overall stress/mental-health outcomes | Overall pooled effect d≈0.53; high heterogeneity (I²≈73%); few absenteeism/productivity studies (pqac-00000015) | Not summarized as a single follow-up estimate in excerpt | Supports moderate overall benefit of stress-management interventions, but evidence on absenteeism/productivity is sparse (pqac-00000015) |
| van der Klink / Klink et al. 2001 | Meta-analysis of 48 experimental studies of work-related stress interventions | Cognitive-behavioural, multimodal, relaxation, organizational | Complaints, psychological resources/responses, quality of work life | CBT and multimodal interventions showed moderate effects; organizational interventions non-significant; excerpt does not provide exact pooled d by CBT subgroup (pqac-00000013) | Not specified in excerpt | Stronger evidence for individual than organizational outcomes; work-outcome estimates not detailed (pqac-00000013) |
| Bhui et al. 2012 | Review of 23 systematic reviews / 499 primary studies | Individual interventions including CBT | Anxiety, depression, absenteeism | CBT programs repeatedly showed larger individual-level mental-health effects than other approaches; no numeric pooled CBT estimate in excerpt (pqac-00000014) | Review-level synthesis; no single durability estimate | Evidence for absenteeism is limited; organizational interventions mixed (pqac-00000014) |
| Joyce et al. 2016 | Systematic meta-review of workplace interventions for common mental disorders | CBT-based workplace and RTW-focused interventions | Symptom reduction, absenteeism, productivity, RTW | Consistent symptom reduction; one cited Cochrane review suggested earlier RTW by ~2 weeks; occupational effects otherwise mixed (pqac-00000009, pqac-00000029) | Depends on underlying reviews | Work-focused / RTW-oriented CBT appears more likely to improve occupational outcomes than generic CBT (pqac-00000009, pqac-00000029) |
| Ryan et al. 2017 | Systematic review of web-based workplace stress interventions | Web-based CBT / iCBT / blended CBT | Stress, mental health, insomnia, work functioning | Across trials, effects ranged from about d=0.3 to >1.0; examples include perceived stress d=0.96 post and d=0.65 at 6 months in GET.ON Stress-related trials (pqac-00000011, pqac-00000016) | Some studies reported 6-month maintenance; uncontrolled 3-year follow-up in one trial (pqac-00000011, pqac-00000022) | Work effects inconsistent; many studies found no absenteeism/presenteeism benefit, though some showed faster RTW or small work-performance gains (pqac-00000011, pqac-00000016, pqac-00000021) |
| Xu et al. 2024 | Systematic review and meta-analysis of 34 RCTs in employees on sick leave | CBT-based interventions, including stress-management and RTW-oriented variants | Sick leave, RTW, depression, stress/anxiety, work ability | Sick leave reduced by MD −3.654 days (95% CI −5.253 to −2.046); RTW ~1.5 days earlier; depression small benefit (effect sizes ~−0.176 to −0.196); no pooled effect for stress, anxiety, or working ability (pqac-00000008, pqac-00000010, pqac-00000025, pqac-00000028, pqac-00000032) | Pooled across varied follow-up intervals; figure/table extracted from review (pqac-00000032, pqac-00000033) | Best quantitative evidence for occupational outcomes, but heterogeneity is high for sick-leave reduction (pqac-00000025, pqac-00000032) |
| Ebert 2016 | RCT in employees with elevated perceived stress (N=264) | Self-guided internet/mobile CBT-based stress management | Perceived stress, depression/anxiety, emotional exhaustion, presenteeism, absenteeism | Perceived stress d=0.96 post, d=0.65 at 6 months; secondary outcomes improved with small-to-moderate effects (pqac-00000018, pqac-00000030) | 6-month follow-up with maintained moderate stress benefit (pqac-00000018, pqac-00000030) | Improved presenteeism, but no significant absenteeism effect at 6 months (pqac-00000018, pqac-00000030) |
| Lindsäter 2018 | RCT in clinical stress-related disorders / adjustment or exhaustion disorder (N=100) | Therapist-guided internet CBT | Perceived stress, secondary stress-related symptoms, work ability | Large stress reduction vs waitlist, d=1.09; moderate-to-large secondary symptom improvements (pqac-00000005, pqac-00000020) | Effects maintained at 6 months (pqac-00000005, pqac-00000020) | No significant effect on work ability or functional impairment (pqac-00000005, pqac-00000020) |
| Ruwaard 2007 | RCT in adults with work-related stress (n=239 randomized) | 7-week e-mailed standardized CBT | Stress, depression, anxiety, emotional exhaustion | Within-group effects ~d=0.5–0.9; between-group effects ~d=0.1–0.6, largest for stress (pqac-00000004, pqac-00000023) | Follow-up suggested more pronounced effects, but attrition limited interpretation (pqac-00000004, pqac-00000023) | No work-specific outcomes reported in extracted sections (pqac-00000004, pqac-00000023) |
| Asplund 2023 | RCT in employees with stress-related disorders (N=182) | Work-focused iCBT vs generic iCBT vs waitlist | Burnout, work ability, sickness absence | Burnout post: d=1.00 (work-focused) and 0.83 (generic); both d=0.74 at 6 months; only work-focused iCBT improved work ability and reduced short-term sickness absence (445 fewer days vs waitlist; 324 fewer vs generic iCBT) (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000017, pqac-00000026) | 6-month benefits maintained; 12-month assessment mentioned in trial (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000017) | Strong evidence that adding work-focused components may improve short-term occupational outcomes beyond symptom relief (pqac-00000017, pqac-00000026) |
| Salomonsson 2020 | Randomized trial in primary-care patients on sick leave; stress subgroup with adjustment/exhaustion disorder (n=152 within larger N=211 trial) | Face-to-face CBT vs RTW intervention vs combined | Symptom severity, registry sick leave | CBT reduced symptoms more than RTW intervention posttreatment; no numeric effect size in excerpt; no sick-leave difference between groups (pqac-00000019, pqac-00000024) | Posttreatment and registry follow-up reported in trial, but no compact numeric durability estimate in excerpt | Illustrates common pattern: symptom benefit without clear added sick-leave benefit for CBT alone (pqac-00000019, pqac-00000024) |


*Table: This table summarizes major peer-reviewed reviews and randomized trials on CBT for workplace or work-related stress. It highlights that CBT generally improves stress and related symptoms, while work outcomes such as absenteeism and return-to-work improve less consistently unless interventions are explicitly work-focused.*
